answer:
There isn’t, as far as I know, a clean study of the exact form: “people choosing a more interesting AI research job vs. a slightly higher-paid/team-lead job: who is happier 5 years later?” But there is a lot of adjacent research on **job characteristics, intrinsic motivation, meaningful work, pay, promotion, and regret**. The results are surprisingly consistent.

## Short version

If the salary difference is only “a bit higher” and you are already financially comfortable, the literature generally suggests that **day-to-day work content, autonomy, skill use, meaning, and fit** predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than marginal increases in pay. However, **leading a team** is not merely an extrinsic perk: if management/mentoring/growing people is intrinsically motivating to you, it may itself be a source of meaning, autonomy, competence, and identity.

So the research-backed framing is not “interesting work vs. salary.” It is more like:

> Which option gives me more of the things that reliably predict long-run satisfaction: autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaningfulness, skill use, growth, and sustainable demands?

## 1. Interesting/challenging work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A large literature under the **Job Characteristics Model** studies features such as:

- autonomy  
- skill variety  
- task significance  
- task identity  
- feedback from the work itself  
- meaningfulness  
- challenge  
- use of one’s abilities

Classic paper: Hackman & Oldham, 1976. Later meta-analyses include Fried & Ferris, 1987, and Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.

The broad finding: jobs that give people **autonomy, variety, feedback, significance, and skill use** are associated with higher:

- job satisfaction  
- internal motivation  
- engagement  
- performance  
- lower turnover intentions

The correlations are often moderate to large by organizational psychology standards. Autonomy and meaningfulness tend to be especially important.

“Interesting work” is not always measured directly, but it overlaps heavily with **skill variety, challenge, autonomy, learning, and meaningfulness**. So yes: the research generally supports the idea that people in more intrinsically engaging jobs tend to be more satisfied.

## 2. Meaningful work has a large association with satisfaction and well-being

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**. A useful review/meta-analysis is Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019.

Meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- organizational commitment  
- lower withdrawal intentions  
- general life satisfaction, though less strongly than job satisfaction

The key point: people do not merely want comfort or compensation. They often want to feel that their work matters, expresses their values, and uses their capacities.

For someone in frontier AI research, “interesting” may also mean “consequential,” “technically deep,” or “connected to a mission.” Those are exactly the kinds of features that tend to predict higher work satisfaction.

## 3. Person–job fit is very important

A major meta-analysis by Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction and negatively related to intent to quit.

This matters because there is no universal answer. Some people genuinely get more satisfaction from:

- mentoring  
- setting direction  
- building teams  
- coordinating work  
- having broader leverage  
- being accountable for outcomes

Others strongly prefer:

- deep technical work  
- research taste  
- individual problem-solving  
- low-meeting environments  
- freedom to explore

So the relevant question is not just “which job is more interesting in the abstract?” but:

> Which role better fits the activities and identity I want to inhabit every week?

If the OpenAI role is “less interesting” technically but highly satisfying interpersonally/strategically because you would lead people, that could offset the loss. If management is mostly coordination, meetings, hiring, and performance review, and you primarily love research itself, the fit may be worse despite the title.

## 4. Pay matters, but marginal pay matters less once needs are met

Pay absolutely matters, especially when it affects security, debt, family obligations, geographic freedom, or perceived fairness. But the marginal relationship between salary and job satisfaction is weaker than many people expect.

A meta-analysis by Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, found that **pay level has only a modest association with job satisfaction**. Pay satisfaction is more related to job attitudes than absolute pay, but still not usually as central as work content, autonomy, and fit.

On general subjective well-being, the income literature is nuanced:

- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation continued to rise with income.  
- Killingsworth, 2021: found happiness continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically, with no sharp plateau.  
- Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023: reconciled some of the disagreement; income keeps helping many people, but effects are heterogeneous and diminishing.

The practical interpretation is:

> A 10–20% salary increase can matter, but if you are already well-compensated, it is often less predictive of day-to-day happiness than the work you actually do for 40–60 hours/week.

Pay also has symbolic effects: recognition, fairness, status. If you feel underpaid relative to peers, that can hurt satisfaction. But “a bit more money” usually does not compensate indefinitely for a large loss of intrinsic motivation.

## 5. Promotions and leadership are mixed: more status/autonomy, but also more stress

Promotion/leadership is not automatically good or bad for well-being. The research is mixed.

Some findings from panel studies suggest promotions can increase job satisfaction, status, income, and sense of control. But they can also increase:

- stress  
- hours  
- responsibility  
- interpersonal conflict  
- administrative load  
- loss of hands-on technical work

For example, work by Boyce & Oswald and by Johnston & Lee on promotions suggests that higher status can come with higher stress, and health/well-being effects are not uniformly positive.

So “leading a small team” should be decomposed. It is good if it gives you:

- more autonomy  
- more leverage  
- more mentoring/teaching  
- more ownership  
- more meaningful responsibility  
- a path you truly want

It is risky if it mostly gives you:

- more meetings  
- less research  
- less intellectual stimulation  
- more politics  
- accountability without control  
- managerial work you like in theory but not in daily practice

The research would not say “take the team lead role” or “avoid management.” It would say: evaluate whether the leadership role satisfies autonomy, competence, relatedness, and meaning.

## 6. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Meta-analytic work, e.g. Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989, and Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010, finds a moderate relationship between **job satisfaction and life satisfaction**. The direction likely runs both ways: happier people enjoy jobs more, and better jobs make people happier.

This is important because people sometimes treat work as “just work.” But for high-skill professionals, work often occupies a large share of waking life and identity. If the daily work becomes boring or misaligned, that can affect overall well-being.

## 7. Regret research gives weaker guidance

The regret literature is less directly helpful.

Roese & Summerville, 2005, found that people commonly report major regrets around education, career, romance, and parenting. Gilovich & Medvec, 1995, found that in the long run people often regret **inactions** more than actions.

But this does not directly answer your case, because both options have possible regret narratives:

- If you stay: “I missed a chance to lead a team and grow into management.”  
- If you leave: “I gave up unusually interesting work for a less compelling role.”  

The regret literature mostly suggests that people regret choices more when they feel they violated their own values, avoided a meaningful challenge, or failed to pursue an identity they cared about. It does not say that people usually regret choosing intrinsic work over pay, or vice versa.

## My synthesis of the research

The strongest evidence says:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work matters a lot.**  
   It is one of the more reliable predictors of job satisfaction.

2. **Marginal salary increases matter, but usually less than people expect once comfortable.**  
   Especially if the raise is modest and does not change life constraints.

3. **Leadership can be deeply satisfying if it matches your motives.**  
   Management is positive when it increases autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth; negative when it mainly adds stress/admin and removes the work you love.

4. **Fit beats generic prestige.**  
   The best predictor is not whether a role is objectively better, but whether the role fits your preferences, strengths, values, and desired identity.

5. **Regret evidence is ambiguous.**  
   People regret career choices, but research does not clearly favor “take the higher-status job” or “stay with more interesting work.”

A research-backed way to make the decision would be to ignore the headline labels and compare the expected weekly experience:

| Dimension | Question |
|---|---|
| Intrinsic interest | Would I be excited by the actual problems? |
| Autonomy | How much control would I have over direction and methods? |
| Skill use | Would I use and grow my strongest abilities? |
| Meaning/mission | Would I believe the work matters? |
| Relatedness | Would I like the people and feel respected? |
| Leadership fit | Do I actually enjoy coaching, coordinating, and being accountable for others? |
| Demands | Would stress/hours be sustainable? |
| Optionality | Which choice preserves or creates future paths I value? |
| Pay/security | Does the salary difference materially change my life, or mostly my sense of status/fairness? |

If the OpenAI role is only slightly better paid but substantially less interesting, the literature would generally caution against overweighting the salary. If the team-lead aspect is genuinely central to the person you want to become, then the “less interesting” label may be misleading: the role may be interesting along a different dimension.

Key papers/search terms:

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976 — Job Characteristics Model  
- Fried & Ferris, 1987 — meta-analysis of job characteristics  
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 — work design meta-analysis  
- Deci & Ryan, 2000 — Self-Determination Theory  
- Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005 — person–job fit meta-analysis  
- Judge et al., 2010 — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
- Allan et al., 2019 — meaningful work meta-analysis  
- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010; Killingsworth, 2021; Killingsworth et al., 2023 — income and well-being  
- Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010 — job satisfaction and subjective well-being  
- Roese & Summerville, 2005; Gilovich & Medvec, 1995 — regret research

answer:
Short answer from the literature: **“interesting/meaningful/autonomous work” is one of the strongest and most reliable predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being; pay matters, but its marginal association with happiness/job satisfaction is usually much smaller once basic needs and fairness are covered.** However, **regret research adds a wrinkle**: people often regret *missed opportunities* in career/education domains, so a rare chance to lead a team could matter if it is genuinely important to your identity/goals.

## 1. Job content and “interesting work” matter a lot

The closest research constructs are **intrinsic motivation**, **person–job fit**, **work design**, **meaningful work**, and **autonomy/competence/relatedness**.

### Work design: autonomy, variety, significance, feedback

The classic **Job Characteristics Model** says jobs are more motivating/satisfying when they have:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** found that these work-design features have moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. Autonomy, task significance, feedback, problem solving, and social support are especially important.

Roughly: if “interesting work” means *hard problems, autonomy, learning, real impact, and feedback*, then the research says it is highly predictive of satisfaction.

### Person–job fit

A meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction — stronger than many traditional predictors. Person–job fit includes whether your abilities, interests, and needs match the role.

This is highly relevant here: if Anthropic gives you unusually good fit with your curiosity and skills, that is not a small factor.

### Interest congruence

Vocational psychology has studied whether people are happier when their work matches their interests. Meta-analyses such as **Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993** and later work generally find a positive but more modest relationship between interest congruence and job satisfaction, often around small-to-moderate correlations.

The relationship is not enormous, partly because “interest” is only one part of work fit — manager, autonomy, culture, workload, compensation, and coworkers all matter too. But the direction is very consistent: people tend to be more satisfied when work fits their interests.

### Meaningful work

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. 2019** found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower withdrawal intentions.

For research roles, “interesting” and “meaningful” may be intertwined: if the work feels intellectually alive and important, that is a substantial well-being predictor.

## 2. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect — especially at high income

The most relevant job-specific result is **Judge et al. 2010**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction. They found that **actual pay level is only weakly related to job satisfaction**, with correlations around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but that is about perceived fairness, comparison, expectations, and financial security — not just absolute salary.

On general happiness/income:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that life evaluation keeps rising with income, but daily emotional well-being showed a plateau around $75k in 2010 dollars.
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the plateau result, finding experienced well-being continues to rise with income.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled the two: for less happy people, emotional well-being may plateau at high incomes; for happier people, it often continues rising, but roughly log-linearly.

For your case, since both jobs are presumably well above financial-security thresholds, the relevant question is probably not “does money matter?” but:

> What concrete life improvement does the salary delta buy me, after tax, relative to the loss/gain in daily work quality?

The literature would generally predict that a moderate salary bump is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in autonomy, meaning, curiosity, or fit — but could compensate for a small drop, especially if the extra money materially changes your options.

## 3. Leading a team is not just an “extrinsic” factor

You framed the OpenAI role as “less interesting but leading a small team, which I always wanted.” The research would treat that leadership opportunity as potentially intrinsically motivating too.

Leadership can increase:

- autonomy,
- impact,
- task significance,
- skill variety,
- social connection,
- identity/status,
- learning/growth.

Those are all predictors of satisfaction.

But management can also increase:

- meetings,
- interpersonal conflict,
- responsibility without control,
- role ambiguity,
- pressure,
- less time doing deep technical work.

So the key is not “individual contributor interesting work vs management title.” The evidence-based framing is:

> Which role has better expected work design: autonomy, learning, meaningfulness, competence growth, social support, values fit, and sustainable demands?

If leading a small team gives you real ownership over important problems, it may be highly satisfying even if the nominal project area is less fascinating. If it mostly gives you coordination overhead on work you do not care about, the title and salary may fade quickly.

## 4. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Meta-analyses such as **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang 2010** find a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being/life satisfaction. The causal direction is partly reciprocal: happier people tend to be more satisfied at work, and satisfying work also contributes to life satisfaction.

Given how much waking time work occupies, day-to-day job quality is not a minor variable.

## 5. Regret: less direct evidence, but relevant patterns

There is less direct research on: “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job instead of taking more money/management?” But regret research has some relevant findings.

### People often regret missed opportunities

**Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that short-term regrets are often about actions taken, but long-term regrets are often about **inactions** — things not tried, paths not explored.

### Career is a major regret domain

**Roese & Summerville 2005** reviewed regret studies and found that education and career are among the most common life-regret domains. Their “opportunity principle” says people regret most those domains where they saw substantial opportunity.

Applied here: if leading a team is something you have long wanted and this feels like a rare window, declining it could become a salient “path not taken.” But the literature does **not** say that every prestigious/higher-paid opportunity should be taken. It says missed opportunities are psychologically sticky when they remain unresolved and counterfactually vivid.

A useful question is:

> If I decline this, will I still have a credible path to team leadership within 1–3 years?

If yes, regret risk is lower. If no, the leadership opportunity deserves more weight.

## 6. Research-based takeaway for your decision

The evidence points to these principles:

1. **Do not undervalue interesting, meaningful work.**  
   It is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, especially when it includes autonomy, challenge, learning, and impact.

2. **Do not overweight salary once both options are financially comfortable.**  
   Pay matters, but the marginal happiness effect of a raise is usually smaller than the effect of daily work quality, manager quality, autonomy, and fit.

3. **Treat “leading a team” as a real intrinsic motivator if it reflects a deep goal.**  
   It may provide autonomy, growth, identity, and impact — all highly relevant. But verify the actual job content.

4. **Regret risk is mostly about unique, identity-relevant missed opportunities.**  
   If team leadership is a long-standing aspiration and hard to get elsewhere, that matters. If it is mostly a title/status bump, research suggests it may adapt quickly.

5. **Compare actual weekdays, not headlines.**  
   Ask: six months in, what fraction of my time is deep work, mentoring, strategy, meetings, firefighting, alignment work, hiring, performance management, etc.?

A compact evidence-based decision rule would be:

> Prefer the role with the better combination of autonomy, meaning, learning, competence growth, social support, and values fit — unless the salary difference materially changes your life or the leadership opportunity is uniquely important and unlikely to recur.

Key references: Hackman & Oldham 1976; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007; Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005; Judge et al. 2010; Allan et al. 2019; Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989; Bowling et al. 2010; Kahneman & Deaton 2010; Killingsworth 2021; Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023; Gilovich & Medvec 1995; Roese & Summerville 2005.

It's important to note that studies offer averages, especially concerning unique positions in AI. If the only perks are a slightly higher salary and title, but they lead to less interest in daily work, the evidence suggests I should remain in a more fulfilling role.
**Assessing team lead roles**

I'm contemplating whether a team lead position could enhance my autonomy, impact, and skill growth to the point where it becomes intrinsically interesting, even if the specific projects aren’t as engaging. It's crucial to keep this evaluation focused on the role itself, not making it too personal. I can reference studies like Fried & Ferris (1987) and Loher et al. (1985) for job characteristics, along with more recent meta-analyses from Humphrey et al. (2007) and others to support my thoughts.
**Evaluating job happiness and regret**

I’m noticing that people tend to be happier in jobs they find interesting, autonomous, or meaningful, which reinforces job satisfaction and overall well-being based on longitudinal data. There’s not much direct evidence about regret, but many people wish they’d pursued more meaningful goals. It seems that pay might weigh less compared to person-job fit, and modest salary increases have diminishing returns. I’ll summarize this while ensuring citations and moderate detail in my final answer.
**Considering regret in decisions**

Regret often comes from options you've passed up, particularly those with clear outcomes like a high salary or title. Sometimes, rejecting an option may lead to periodic regret. I could suggest a "minimum viable decision experiment" that focuses on evidence-based inference for making your trade-offs.

For instance, a small salary increase might not significantly impact happiness for high earners. Leading a small team could give autonomy and broader impact, while less interesting work may have a larger downside. The key question is whether team leadership makes the work more engaging for you.
answer:
I’ll focus on research rather than advice. The short version is:

**Yes: across several literatures, “interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work” is one of the strongest and most reliable predictors of job satisfaction, engagement, and lower turnover.** Salary matters, but the marginal relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is usually much smaller, especially once basic financial/security needs are met. However, “leading a small team” may itself be a source of autonomy, meaning, competence, status, and growth — so the real comparison is not “interesting work vs leadership,” but whether the new role would still satisfy the underlying psychological drivers that make work rewarding.

## 1. How researchers operationalize “interesting work”

The literature usually does not use the phrase “interesting work” directly. It studies related constructs:

- **Intrinsic motivation**: doing work because the activity itself is engaging.
- **Job characteristics / work design**: autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, feedback.
- **Person–job fit**: how well the role matches your interests, abilities, values.
- **Meaningful work / calling**: work experienced as purposeful or significant.
- **Psychological need satisfaction**: autonomy, competence, relatedness — from self-determination theory.

These map fairly well onto “I am doing work I find intellectually alive, important, and self-directed.”

## 2. Job content is a major predictor of job satisfaction

A large literature based on the **Job Characteristics Model** — Hackman & Oldham — finds that jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they have:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- task identity,
- feedback,
- opportunities to use valued abilities.

Meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris 1987** and especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** find that these motivational job characteristics are robustly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction,
- higher internal motivation,
- higher organizational commitment,
- lower turnover intentions,
- lower burnout.

The correlations are often in the moderate-to-large range for organizational psychology — roughly the size you would expect from factors that meaningfully affect day-to-day life. Autonomy and task significance are especially important.

**Decision-relevant interpretation:** if one role gives you substantially more intellectual engagement, autonomy, and sense of importance, the research would predict higher job satisfaction, all else equal.

## 3. Person–job fit is very important

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction. The reported meta-analytic correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction is around **r ≈ .56**, which is large by social-science standards. It is also associated with lower intent to quit and higher commitment.

In plain English: people tend to be much happier when the actual substance of the role fits their interests, strengths, and values.

This seems especially relevant for research roles. If your strongest source of energy is working on technically deep, frontier research, a move away from that could be costly unless the new role provides other forms of fit — e.g. technical strategy, mentoring, agenda-setting, scientific leadership.

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly linked to satisfaction and well-being

There is also a literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analyses, e.g. **Allan et al. 2019**, generally find that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction,
- engagement,
- life satisfaction,
- lower withdrawal intentions,
- sometimes lower depression/burnout, depending on context.

The effect sizes are usually larger than those seen for pay level. Meaningful work is not always purely positive — people with a strong “calling” may overwork or accept worse conditions — but on average, meaning is a major contributor to work well-being.

Relatedly, **Wrzesniewski et al. 1997** distinguished people who view work as a “job,” “career,” or “calling.” Those who experienced their work as a calling tended to report higher satisfaction and life meaning, though the direction of causality is not always clean.

## 5. Salary matters, but less than people often expect

A key meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They found that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**, roughly **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction itself is more related to job satisfaction than absolute pay, but still not usually as large as fit, autonomy, or meaningful work.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. Pay matters more when it affects:

- financial security,
- debt/stress,
- ability to support family,
- perceived fairness,
- status or recognition,
- future optionality.

But for someone already well-compensated, a “bit higher salary” would usually be predicted to have a relatively small effect on daily happiness compared with a large shift in the nature of the work.

On broader life satisfaction, the income literature is nuanced. **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found a plateau-ish pattern for emotional well-being at moderate-high income, while **Killingsworth 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** found that well-being continues to rise with income for many people, roughly with log income. The shared implication is still diminishing marginal returns: a given percentage raise matters less at high income than at low income.

## 6. Intrinsic motivation tends to matter more for sustained engagement

Self-determination theory — **Deci & Ryan** — argues that people are more motivated and well when work satisfies:

- **autonomy**: I have agency;
- **competence**: I am using and developing abilities;
- **relatedness**: I am connected to others;
- **purpose/values alignment**: often added in work applications.

Workplace meta-analyses, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, find that satisfaction of these needs is associated with higher engagement, well-being, and performance, and lower burnout.

There is also evidence from **Deci, Koestner & Ryan 1999** that external rewards can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks when rewards feel controlling. This should not be overinterpreted — salary does not automatically make work less meaningful — but it supports the broader point that feeling autonomous and intrinsically engaged is central.

## 7. What about leading a team?

The research does not say “being an IC researcher is better” or “being a manager is better.” It would ask what the leadership role changes in the underlying job characteristics.

Leading a small team could increase:

- autonomy,
- influence,
- task significance,
- mentoring satisfaction,
- strategic scope,
- career capital,
- identity fulfillment.

But it could also increase:

- meetings,
- coordination load,
- personnel conflict,
- accountability without control,
- distance from hands-on research,
- stress and ambiguity.

Promotion studies generally find that promotions often increase job satisfaction, at least initially, but some effects attenuate over time as people adapt and as new demands become salient. So the title/status component may be less durable than the day-to-day work content.

The research-based question is: **Would leading the team make the new role intrinsically interesting in a different way, or would it mostly be less interesting work plus more responsibility?**

That distinction matters a lot.

## 8. Do people regret choosing interesting work over money/status?

There is less direct research on this exact question. I do not know of clean longitudinal studies that ask: “People chose the more interesting job over the better-paid/status job; did they later regret it?”

But related evidence is suggestive.

### Affective forecasting

Research by **Gilbert, Wilson**, and others on affective forecasting shows that people often mispredict what will make them happy. They overweight salient, easy-to-compare features and underweight repeated daily experiences.

### Distinction bias

**Hsee & Zhang 2004** on “distinction bias” shows that when people compare options side-by-side, they overweight attributes that are easy to compare numerically or symbolically. Salary and title are very easy to compare. Daily intellectual engagement is harder to compare but may matter more once you are actually living the job.

Applied cautiously: in the offer-comparison moment, “higher salary” and “team lead” may loom larger than they will in day-to-day life, while “I spend most of my time on less interesting work” may loom smaller than it will later.

### Regret research

**Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that people’s regrets often shift over time: short-term regrets are more often about actions taken; long-term regrets are often about inactions or missed identity-relevant opportunities. But this cuts both ways here. You might later regret not taking a leadership opportunity if team-building is central to your ideal self. Or you might regret leaving unusually meaningful research if that is central to your identity.

So the regret literature does not give a simple answer. It suggests looking at which option is more connected to your long-term identity and values, not just which has the better external markers.

## 9. Best empirical takeaway for your case

Based on the research, I would summarize the evidence like this:

1. **A large decrease in work interestingness/meaning is a serious cost.** It is not a “soft” factor; it is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.

2. **A modest salary increase is probably a smaller happiness lever**, especially at already-high income, unless it changes financial security, fairness, or life options.

3. **Leadership could be a real positive**, not merely status, if it gives you autonomy, competence growth, mentoring satisfaction, and broader impact.

4. **The decisive variable is likely daily role content.** What will you actually do hour by hour? Research, strategy, people management, roadmapping, politics, recruiting, evaluation, meetings?

5. **People often overweight salary/title in advance and underweight lived experience.** This is one of the more decision-relevant findings from affective forecasting and distinction-bias research.

A compact evidence-based framing would be:

> If the OpenAI role is “less interesting work, plus a bit more salary and a title,” the literature would predict a meaningful risk of lower satisfaction.  
> If it is “less hands-on research, but more autonomy, mentoring, strategic influence, and personally meaningful leadership,” then it may satisfy the same psychological needs through a different route.

So the research does not imply “always choose the more interesting job.” It implies: **choose the role with the better expected combination of autonomy, meaning, competence growth, fit, and sustainable daily experience — and be skeptical that modest pay/title improvements will compensate for losing those.**

answer:
Yes. The relevant research is mostly under **job characteristics / work design**, **intrinsic motivation**, **person–job fit**, **meaningful work**, and **income vs subjective well-being**. There is not much clean evidence on “people who chose the more interesting job later regretted it,” but there is a lot on which job attributes predict satisfaction and well-being.

## Short summary

Research generally suggests:

1. **The work itself matters a lot.** Autonomy, intellectual challenge, skill use, task variety, meaning, and fit with interests are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.

2. **Pay matters, but marginal pay matters less once you are already financially comfortable.** Pay is reliably associated with life satisfaction, but with diminishing returns; in job-satisfaction studies, pay is usually a weaker predictor than the nature of the work, autonomy, manager/team quality, and work-life factors.

3. **“Leading a team” is not merely extrinsic.** Management can provide autonomy, mastery, status, mentoring, and impact — all of which can be intrinsically rewarding — but it can also replace deep technical work with coordination, politics, and meetings. The research would frame this as a change in job design, not just title.

4. **Regret evidence is indirect.** People often overweight salient, easily comparable factors like salary/title and underweight day-to-day experience. Long-run regrets often concern missed opportunities, self-congruence, or inaction, but there is no simple result saying “choose interest over salary” or vice versa.

## Main research results

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics strongly predict job satisfaction

The classic **Job Characteristics Model** — Hackman & Oldham — argues that jobs are more motivating when they provide:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

Meta-analyses broadly support this. For example, **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** reviewed a large work-design literature and found that motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, and job complexity have substantial relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance.

A rough takeaway: variables like **autonomy**, **skill variety**, **task significance**, and **social support** often correlate with job satisfaction in the **moderate to strong** range, commonly around `.3–.5` depending on the measure and study.

So if by “interesting work” you mean:

- intellectually challenging,
- high learning rate,
- high autonomy,
- using your strongest skills,
- connected to something you find important,

then the literature treats that as central, not peripheral.

### 2. Person–job fit and interest fit are important

Meta-analyses on **person–environment fit** find that fit between a person and their job/organization predicts job satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions.

A useful citation: **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, a meta-analysis of person–job, person–organization, person–group, and person–supervisor fit. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.

Vocational-interest research, e.g. work following **Holland’s RIASEC model**, also finds that people tend to be more satisfied and perform better when their work matches their interests, though effects are usually moderate rather than enormous.

For your situation, “this work is much more interesting to me” maps fairly directly onto **person–job fit** and **interest congruence**.

### 3. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

A related but distinct construct is **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic evidence suggests meaningful work is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction,
- engagement,
- organizational commitment,
- lower turnover intentions,
- sometimes broader life satisfaction.

See e.g. **Allan et al. 2019, “Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis.”**

One caveat: “calling” or meaningful work can also make people tolerate worse conditions or overwork. So meaningful/interesting work is good, but not if it comes bundled with chronic stress, lack of boundaries, or exploitation.

### 4. Pay predicts happiness, but less than people often expect — especially at high income

There are two separate questions:

#### Pay and job satisfaction

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al. 2010** found that the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is positive but relatively modest — around `r ≈ .15` in many analyses. Pay satisfaction matters more than absolute pay, but even then it is not usually the dominant driver of overall job satisfaction.

Their broad conclusion was that if the goal is satisfied employees, money helps but is not the whole story and is often weaker than work design and social factors.

#### Income and general well-being

On income and subjective well-being:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to roughly $75k/year in 2010 US dollars, while life evaluation kept rising beyond that.
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the plateau result, finding experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly log-linearly.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled some of the disagreement: for less-happy people there may be a plateau at higher incomes, but for many people well-being continues rising with income, though with diminishing marginal returns.

The safest summary: **more money helps, but the effect is logarithmic/diminishing, and a marginal raise at already-high income usually has a smaller effect than people predict unless it changes real constraints: housing, family security, debt, retirement, freedom, etc.**

For someone already highly paid in frontier AI, a “bit higher salary” may not move experienced well-being much unless it meaningfully changes your financial options.

### 5. Workers demonstrably trade pay for nonwage job attributes

Labor economists have studied how much compensation workers are willing to sacrifice for job amenities.

For example:

- **Mas & Pallais 2017, “Valuing Alternative Work Arrangements”**: workers are willing to trade wages for schedule control and other nonwage attributes, though valuations vary a lot.
- **Maestas et al. 2018**, on the value of working conditions, found that workers place substantial value on nonwage job characteristics such as autonomy, schedule, physical demands, and meaning.

There is also a nice scientist-specific line of work:

- **Stern 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** found evidence that scientists accept lower wages for jobs that allow them to engage more in science/open research.
- Related work by **Roach and Sauermann** finds that scientists differ strongly in preferences for autonomy, publishing, pay, and commercialization; those preferences affect career choices.

This is directly relevant: research-oriented people often do accept lower pay for more intellectually satisfying or scientifically autonomous work, and this is not obviously irrational.

### 6. Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central

**Self-Determination Theory**, associated with Deci and Ryan, says that well-being and intrinsic motivation depend heavily on three needs:

- autonomy,
- competence,
- relatedness.

Workplace reviews/meta-analyses, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, generally support the idea that satisfaction of these needs at work predicts well-being, engagement, and performance.

Interesting research work often satisfies autonomy and competence. Leading a small team may satisfy competence, relatedness, and autonomy — or may reduce autonomy if the role is mostly execution, coordination, and upward management.

So the relevant question is not just “IC research vs manager role,” but:

> Which role gives me more autonomy, mastery, relatedness, and purpose on an ordinary week?

### 7. Regret evidence is indirect, but relevant

There is not much direct evidence of the form: “People who chose the more interesting lower-paid job later regretted/did not regret it.”

But several adjacent findings matter.

#### People are imperfect at predicting what will make them happy

Work on **affective forecasting** — Wilson & Gilbert — shows that people often mispredict the intensity and duration of future feelings.

Work by **Hsee & Hastie 2006**, “Decision and Experience,” argues that people often overvalue attributes that are easy to compare at decision time and undervalue attributes that dominate lived experience.

Salary and title are highly comparable. Daily intellectual engagement is harder to compare but may matter more once you are living the job.

#### Long-term regrets often involve missed opportunities and inaction

Research by **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** and **Roese & Summerville 2005** suggests that long-term regrets often concern things people did not do: missed opportunities, unpursued paths, education/career choices, etc.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- You might regret leaving unusually interesting research.
- You might regret not trying leadership when you had the chance.

So regret research does not give a clean answer, but it suggests paying attention to **option value and reversibility**.

## How I would translate the evidence into your specific tradeoff

Research would suggest the following prior:

> Do not underestimate the value of interesting, autonomous, meaningful work. It is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, and marginal salary increases are usually less powerful once baseline financial needs are well met.

But also:

> Do not classify “leading a small team” as merely extrinsic. If leadership is something you deeply want, and the role gives you autonomy, mentorship, strategy, and ownership, it may itself be intrinsically rewarding.

The key empirical questions are probably:

1. **How much less interesting is the OpenAI work day-to-day?**  
   Is it 20% less intellectually exciting, or does it replace your core motivation?

2. **How much real autonomy would you have?**  
   Autonomy is one of the most consistently important work-design variables.

3. **Would leading the team satisfy a deep intrinsic goal or just provide status?**  
   If you have long wanted to mentor, set direction, and grow people, that matters.

4. **Is the salary difference large enough to change your life constraints?**  
   If it merely increases savings rate a bit, research suggests the well-being effect may be small. If it changes family/security/freedom, it matters more.

5. **Is the move reversible?**  
   Regret is lower when you can treat the move as an experiment rather than a one-way door.

6. **What would a normal Tuesday look like?**  
   The literature is quite consistent that day-to-day experienced utility matters more than people expect at decision time.

## A compact evidence-based heuristic

If the new role is:

- only slightly less interesting,
- much better for autonomy/leadership growth,
- still mission-aligned,
- with good people,
- and the salary/security difference is meaningful,

then the research does not argue against moving.

If the new role is:

- substantially less intellectually alive,
- less autonomous,
- more political/meeting-heavy,
- only modestly better paid,
- and leadership is mostly title rather than real agency,

then the research would put a strong prior on staying with the more interesting work or negotiating for a role that preserves more of it.

## Selected references

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980 — Job Characteristics Model.  
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 — Meta-analysis of work design/job characteristics.  
- Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005 — Person–environment fit meta-analysis.  
- Allan et al., 2019 — Meaningful work meta-analysis.  
- Van den Broeck et al., 2016 — Self-Determination Theory at work review.  
- Judge et al., 2010 — Pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis.  
- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010 — Income and emotional well-being.  
- Killingsworth, 2021; Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023 — Income and experienced well-being.  
- Mas & Pallais, 2017 — Valuing alternative work arrangements.  
- Maestas et al., 2018 — Value of working conditions.  
- Stern, 2004 — “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”  
- Wilson & Gilbert — Affective forecasting.  
- Hsee & Hastie, 2006 — Decision vs experienced utility.  
- Gilovich & Medvec, 1995; Roese & Summerville, 2005 — Regret research.

answer:
There isn’t a clean study of “stay in the more interesting AI research job vs take the higher-paid less-interesting team-lead job,” but there is a lot of adjacent research. The fairly consistent result is:

**Intrinsic job content — interesting work, autonomy, mastery/skill use, meaning, fit with values — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and is more strongly associated with happiness than pay level, especially once pay is already high.**  
But: **leading a team is not merely an extrinsic perk**; it can also add autonomy, growth, impact, responsibility, and social meaning. So the real comparison is not “interesting work vs money/status,” but “which role gives you more of the daily psychological ingredients that predict satisfaction?”

## 1. “Interesting work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under constructs like:

- intrinsic motivation  
- job complexity/challenge  
- skill variety  
- autonomy  
- task significance/meaningfulness  
- person–job fit  
- growth opportunities  
- work identity/calling

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more satisfying when they provide autonomy, feedback, skill variety, task identity, and task significance. Meta-analyses generally support this.

Notable results:

- **Fried & Ferris 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model: core job characteristics were substantially related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design: motivational work characteristics — autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, job complexity — had moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction, motivation, commitment, and lower stress. Autonomy and meaningfulness-type variables are among the strongest predictors.
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, meta-analysis on person–job fit: person–job fit had a large association with job satisfaction, roughly around the `.5` correlation range, and also predicted commitment and lower intent to quit.

So, if by “more interesting” you mean “better intellectual fit, more autonomy, more challenge, more meaning, more use of my strengths,” then yes: the research says that matters a lot.

## 2. Job satisfaction meaningfully affects overall happiness

Job satisfaction is not the same as life satisfaction, but they are strongly related.

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and later meta-analyses found a moderate positive relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction.
- **Bowling et al. 2010**, meta-analysis, also found that job satisfaction and subjective well-being are substantially related, with correlations often in the `.3–.4` range depending on measures.

This is not trivial. Work is a large fraction of waking life, so persistent boredom, frustration, or lack of meaning tends to spill over.

Also, job dissatisfaction is associated with mental-health outcomes:

- **Faragher, Cass & Cooper 2005**, meta-analysis: low job satisfaction was associated with burnout, depression, anxiety, and poorer mental health.

So the evidence supports the common intuition that “liking the work itself” matters for happiness, not only for career performance.

## 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect — especially at high income

Pay is not irrelevant. It affects security, options, status, family life, and perceived fairness. But absolute pay level is usually a weaker predictor of job satisfaction than intrinsic job characteristics.

A key paper:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: pay level was only modestly related to job satisfaction, with correlations around `.15` for pay level and somewhat higher for pay satisfaction. In other words: people who are paid more are somewhat more satisfied, but the relationship is not large.

On income and general well-being:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the sharp plateau idea and found well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being keeps rising with income, but the marginal effect is smaller at higher incomes and varies by baseline happiness.

For someone already highly compensated, the research would usually predict that a moderate salary bump has **less day-to-day happiness impact** than a large difference in work content, autonomy, meaning, or stress — unless the money materially changes your life plans, security, family situation, runway, ability to donate, etc.

## 4. Meaningful work is especially strongly associated with satisfaction

If your current role feels meaningful, not just “fun,” that matters.

- **Allan et al. 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work: meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and moderately associated with life satisfaction and life meaning.

Meaningful work can also have downsides — people may overwork, accept worse conditions, or feel identity-threatened when things go badly — but on average, meaningfulness is one of the more robust positive predictors.

In your case, if “interesting” means “I feel this work matters and fits my values,” that is a stronger signal than mere intellectual entertainment.

## 5. Leadership could compensate — if it is intrinsically motivating for you

The OpenAI offer has two factors: higher salary and leading a small team. The salary is mostly extrinsic. But leading a team may be intrinsically valuable.

Leadership can increase:

- autonomy  
- responsibility  
- task significance  
- social connection  
- mentoring/developing others  
- strategic influence  
- identity growth  
- career capital  

Those are exactly the kinds of job characteristics that predict satisfaction.

But management can also increase:

- meetings  
- interruptions  
- political load  
- emotional labor  
- accountability without control  
- reduced deep technical work  
- stress and work–life conflict  

The **Job Demands–Resources** literature, e.g. **Bakker & Demerouti**, suggests that high-demand jobs are sustainable when they also come with high resources: autonomy, support, control, recovery, and meaning. The older **Karasek demand–control model** similarly suggests that high demands are less harmful when paired with high control; high-demand, low-control jobs are worst.

So the research does not imply “never trade interesting IC work for management.” It implies: ask whether the team-lead role gives you more autonomy, growth, impact, and meaning, or whether it mostly gives you more responsibility plus less intrinsically rewarding work.

## 6. Regret research: people often regret missed opportunities, not just bad moves

The regret literature is relevant but less direct.

Two classic findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.
- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: common life regrets cluster around domains with high perceived opportunity, especially education, career, romance, and parenting. Career regrets are common.

This cuts both ways.

You might later regret **not trying leadership** if leading a team is something you “always wanted to do.”  
But you might also regret leaving unusually meaningful/interesting research if that is central to your identity and long-term goals.

The regret literature does **not** say “people regret choosing interest over money” or “people regret choosing money over interest.” It says regret tends to arise around salient foregone opportunities, especially when people feel they could have acted differently.

## 7. The research-backed synthesis

If I compress the literature into practical conclusions:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**  
   Stronger than pay level in most studies.

2. **Pay matters, but marginal salary increases have diminishing subjective value**, especially if you are already financially secure.

3. **Person–job fit matters a lot.**  
   A technically prestigious job that is a poor fit can still be dissatisfying.

4. **Management can be a source of intrinsic satisfaction**, not merely status.  
   If you genuinely want to lead, mentor, and shape direction, that is a real psychological reward.

5. **A less interesting job with more money is risky if the only compensating factor is money.**  
   But a less technically interesting job with much more autonomy, leadership, growth, and impact may be satisfying.

6. **Regret risk is highest around foregone growth opportunities and identity-relevant choices.**  
   If “leading a team” is a long-standing aspiration, that matters. If “doing this kind of research” is central to your values, that matters too.

A research-consistent way to frame the decision would be to score each option not just on compensation/prestige, but on:

- daily intellectual interest  
- autonomy/control  
- meaning/mission fit  
- opportunity for mastery  
- opportunity for leadership growth  
- quality of collaborators  
- expected stress and meeting load  
- work–life sustainability  
- long-term career optionality  
- reversibility of the move  

The literature would especially warn against overweighting the salary bump if your current compensation is already sufficient. But it would also warn against undervaluing the leadership opportunity if that is a genuine intrinsic goal rather than just a title.

answer:
I’d translate your question into: **how much should I weight intrinsic/motivating work content versus pay/status/leadership opportunity?** The research is not perfect for this exact counterfactual, but there are several relevant literatures.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and well-being research, **the content/design of the work itself is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout**. “Interesting work” usually decomposes into autonomy, skill use, learning, variety, meaningfulness, feedback, and person–job fit — all of which predict well-being.

By contrast, **pay matters**, especially for financial security, fairness, and job choice, but meta-analyses generally find that **absolute pay is only weakly to moderately related to job satisfaction**, and the marginal effect of more pay diminishes once you are already financially comfortable.

The evidence would roughly suggest:

> If the salary difference does not materially change your financial security, and the new role is genuinely much less intrinsically engaging, the research would predict a real risk of lower day-to-day satisfaction.  
> But if “leading a small team” is itself a deep intrinsic goal — autonomy, mentoring, leverage, identity, growth — that can compensate, because responsibility/autonomy/meaning are also major satisfaction drivers.

## 1. Work design: autonomy, variety, skill use, meaning predict satisfaction strongly

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model: job characteristics predicted job satisfaction and internal motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design: motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — were strongly related to job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and lower stress/burnout.

For your case, “interesting research work” likely includes several of these: complexity, skill use, novelty, feedback, autonomy, significance. If the OpenAI role is “less interesting” because it has less of those, that is not a small consideration; those are central predictors of job satisfaction.

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit matter

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit**: people are happier and perform better when their job matches their interests, values, and abilities.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis: person–job fit was strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.
- Vocational interest congruence studies, e.g. **Assouline & Meir, 1987** and later meta-analyses, generally find that people whose work fits their interests are more satisfied and somewhat more successful.

The size of the “interest congruence” effect is usually not enormous by itself, but broader person–job fit is quite important. So if your current work is unusually well-matched to your curiosity and abilities, that is a meaningful asset.

## 3. Intrinsic motivation predicts well-being; extrinsic rewards can be weaker

Self-Determination Theory is also relevant. It emphasizes three psychological needs:

- autonomy  
- competence  
- relatedness  

Jobs satisfying those needs tend to produce more motivation and well-being.

- **Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999**, meta-analysis: external rewards can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when experienced as controlling.
- **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**, meta-analysis of basic psychological needs at work: need satisfaction at work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, performance, and lower burnout.

This does not mean “never take the money.” It means that if the higher salary/status comes with reduced autonomy, reduced competence growth, or less meaningful work, the psychological cost can be real.

## 4. Meaningful work is a large predictor of satisfaction and well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research careers they often overlap.

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis: meaningful work was strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction, and lower depression/withdrawal intentions.
- Older work by **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997** on jobs/careers/callings found that people who experience their work as a “calling” tend to report higher satisfaction.

If your current role feels meaningful because the research problems themselves matter to you, that is a significant well-being predictor. But if leading a team would make the work feel more meaningful because you have more leverage and mentorship impact, that could also count.

## 5. Pay matters, but marginal salary increases often predict satisfaction weakly

There is good evidence that pay affects job choice and retention, and people often understate its importance. But the link between pay level and job satisfaction is weaker than many expect.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: pay level was only weakly related to overall job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction mattered more than objective pay, but still was not the dominant factor.
- **Rynes, Gerhart & Minette, 2004** argued that people often say pay is less important than it actually is behaviorally — so self-reports can understate pay’s motivational role.

For general well-being:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** found a more continuous relationship between income and experienced well-being, but still roughly logarithmic: each additional dollar matters less at higher income levels.

In a high-salary research-engineering context, the marginal salary bump may improve life evaluation, security, options, or status, but the research suggests it is unlikely to dominate daily work experience unless it changes something concrete: housing, family plans, financial independence timeline, ability to take risks, etc.

## 6. Leadership opportunity is ambiguous: it could be intrinsically valuable or mostly status

“Leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk. It can increase several satisfaction-relevant dimensions:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- task significance  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- learning and identity growth  
- career capital  

But management can also reduce the features that make technical research satisfying:

- less deep work  
- more coordination overhead  
- more politics  
- more ambiguity  
- responsibility without direct control  
- less time solving the problems you find intrinsically fascinating  

The research does not say “IC work is happier than management” or vice versa. It says to inspect the **actual job characteristics**. A leadership role can be highly motivating if the leadership work itself satisfies autonomy, competence, and meaning. It can be disappointing if it is mostly meetings, alignment, and less contact with the intellectually interesting substrate.

## 7. Regret evidence is thinner

There is less direct evidence on “do people regret staying in the more interesting job instead of taking higher pay/status?” Most regret research is more general.

Relevant findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the long run, people often regret inactions more than actions. This could support trying the leadership role if it represents an important untried path.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: common life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and missed opportunities.
- **Wilson & Gilbert** on affective forecasting: people often mispredict the intensity/duration of future happiness or unhappiness.
- **Schkade & Kahneman, 1998**, “focusing illusion”: when evaluating one factor, people overestimate its importance to overall happiness.

Applied here: you might overfocus on the salient raise/title/team-lead opportunity, or you might overfocus on the current intellectual excitement. The research suggests asking what your **ordinary Tuesday** will feel like, not just what the offer symbolizes.

## Practical research-based way to decide

Instead of comparing “Anthropic vs OpenAI” or “interesting vs salary,” compare the roles on predictors that actually show up in the literature:

| Factor | Evidence-based importance |
|---|---|
| Autonomy | Very important |
| Skill growth/competence | Very important |
| Meaning/task significance | Very important |
| Person–job fit/interests | Important |
| Social support/manager quality | Important |
| Workload/stress/recovery | Important |
| Pay | Important, but marginal effect weaker at high income |
| Status/title | Often less durable unless tied to autonomy/meaning |
| Leadership opportunity | Valuable if intrinsically motivating, not just symbolic |

Questions I’d ask, based on the research:

1. **How much of the new role is actual technical/research judgment vs coordination?**
2. **Will you have real autonomy over agenda, hiring, direction, standards?**
3. **Do you find developing people intrinsically rewarding, or mainly want the career milestone?**
4. **Would the salary difference materially change your life, or mostly your scorekeeping?**
5. **Which role gives you more competence growth over the next 2–3 years?**
6. **If the OpenAI role is less interesting, can you shape it into something interesting?**
7. **If you stay, is there a realistic path to leading a team where the work remains interesting?**

## Bottom line from the literature

The strongest empirical takeaway is:

> People tend to be happier and more engaged when their work is intrinsically motivating, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to their interests. Pay matters, but beyond comfort/security its marginal effect is usually smaller than the day-to-day quality of the work.

So if “much less interesting” really means less autonomy, less learning, less fit, and less meaning, the research would lean toward caution. But if leading a team is itself one of your core intrinsic motivations, then the new role may not actually be “less interesting” in the psychologically relevant sense — it may be interesting along a different dimension.

answer:
Short version: **yes, there is a lot of evidence that “interesting work” / intrinsic motivation / fit / autonomy are major predictors of job satisfaction and well-being, often stronger than marginal pay—especially once pay is already high enough.** But “leading a small team” can itself be intrinsically rewarding if it increases autonomy, impact, learning, and relatedness. Direct evidence on “regret choosing interesting work over salary” is much thinner.

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

The closest research constructs are:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **job complexity / skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaningful work**
- **person–job fit**
- **interest congruence**
- **opportunities for learning and mastery**

A classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** by Hackman & Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating when they provide autonomy, variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback.

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, a large meta-analysis of work-design research, found that motivational job characteristics—autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity—are strongly related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, organizational commitment, and lower strain/turnover intentions.
- Effects are often in the **medium-to-large range** by social-science standards. Autonomy and meaningfulness tend to be especially important.

So if by “more interesting” you mean “more autonomy, more intellectual challenge, more learning, more sense that the work matters,” then yes: the literature strongly suggests those are central to happiness at work.

## 2. Person–job fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit**, especially **person–job fit**: how well the job matches your abilities, interests, and values.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person–job fit correlates strongly with job satisfaction and organizational commitment, and negatively with turnover intentions.
- Approximate corrected correlations between person–job fit and job satisfaction are often reported around **r ≈ .50+**, which is quite large for organizational psychology.

There is also research on **vocational interest congruence**, e.g. whether people whose jobs match their interests are more satisfied. Meta-analyses in that area usually find **modest but reliable positive effects** on satisfaction, performance, and persistence. The effect is not always huge, but it is consistently in the expected direction.

Implication: if your current role is unusually well-matched to your research taste, that is a real asset, not a “soft” factor to ignore.

## 3. Pay matters, but marginal pay usually matters less than people expect

Pay is not irrelevant. It matters for security, fairness, status, options, family goals, etc. But the empirical relationship between pay and job satisfaction is usually weaker than the relationship between job content and satisfaction.

A commonly cited meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction,” found that pay level has only a **modest correlation** with job satisfaction—roughly **r ≈ .15** with overall job satisfaction and somewhat higher with pay satisfaction.

For subjective well-being more broadly, the income literature is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the hard plateau story, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically.
- More recent reconciliations suggest income keeps mattering, but the marginal benefit diminishes and differs across people.

For someone already in a very high-paying field, a “bit higher salary” is likely to have **smaller day-to-day happiness impact** than changes in autonomy, interest, stress, or fit—unless the money changes something concrete for you, e.g. financial independence timeline, family constraints, risk tolerance, location, visa/security, etc.

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

A useful summary:

- **Allan et al. 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work, found that meaningful work is strongly positively related to job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction, and organizational commitment, and negatively related to withdrawal intentions.

For researchers, “interesting” often overlaps with “meaningful”: you are not just doing pleasant tasks, but working on problems you consider important. That combination tends to be especially predictive of engagement.

## 5. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

**Self-Determination Theory**, associated with Deci and Ryan, says people do better when three needs are met:

1. **Autonomy** — I have agency.
2. **Competence** — I am growing and effective.
3. **Relatedness** — I feel connected to others.

Workplace SDT research generally finds that satisfaction of these needs predicts motivation, well-being, performance, and lower burnout.

Relevant to your case:

- More interesting research may satisfy **competence** and **autonomy**.
- Leading a team may satisfy **autonomy**, **relatedness**, **impact**, and status.
- But management can also reduce deep technical work and increase coordination burden, role conflict, and stress.

So “less interesting work but leading a team” is not simply “intrinsic vs extrinsic.” Leadership can be intrinsically motivating if you genuinely want to develop people, set direction, and multiply impact.

## 6. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are linked

Job satisfaction is not the same as happiness, but they are meaningfully connected.

Meta-analytic work, e.g.:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989**
- later work by **Bowling et al.**

finds that job satisfaction and life satisfaction correlate around **r ≈ .40–.50** in many studies. The relationship is partly reciprocal: happier people may like their jobs more, but good/bad jobs also affect life satisfaction.

Given how much waking time people spend at work, daily work quality matters.

## 7. Evidence on regret is weaker

There is less direct research on questions like:

> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paying one?”

But adjacent literatures suggest a few things.

First, career is a common domain of regret:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that education and career are among the most common life regrets.

Second, long-term regrets are often about not pursuing personally meaningful or “ideal self” goals:

- **Davidai & Gilovich 2018** found that regrets related to the “ideal self”—who one wanted to become—can be especially enduring compared with regrets about obligations or duties.

This does not prove that people should always choose interesting work. But it suggests that people may be more haunted by paths not taken when those paths felt central to their identity or values.

In your case, the question might be:  
**Would leaving the more interesting research feel like giving up an important part of the person you want to become?**  
Or would not taking the leadership role feel like failing to pursue a long-standing growth goal?

Both could be “ideal self” concerns.

## 8. Important caveat: research does not say “always choose the interesting job”

The empirical result is not:

> “Interesting work dominates everything.”

Rather, it is closer to:

> “Intrinsic job quality, fit, autonomy, meaning, and growth are among the strongest predictors of work happiness; marginal pay is usually weaker, especially at high income levels.”

Other factors can dominate:

- bad manager
- excessive workload
- ethical/value misalignment
- burnout risk
- low psychological safety
- lack of future growth
- location/family constraints
- career capital
- organizational instability
- whether the new leadership role creates future access to more interesting work

Also, some people value management itself very highly. If leading a small team is something you have “always wanted,” that may be a genuine intrinsic factor, not merely a prestige/salary factor.

## 9. Research-backed way to frame your decision

Based on the literature, I would separate the offer into empirically important dimensions:

| Dimension | Research relevance |
|---|---|
| Intellectual interest | intrinsic motivation, person–job fit |
| Autonomy | one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction |
| Meaning / mission fit | meaningful work, person–organization fit |
| Competence growth | self-determination theory, mastery |
| Leadership opportunity | autonomy, impact, relatedness, identity growth |
| Social environment | relatedness, manager/team support |
| Workload / stress | job demands-resources model |
| Pay | matters, but marginal effect often modest |
| Future option value | career capital, harder to measure but important |

The key empirical question is not simply:

> “Is the new job less interesting?”

but:

> “Does the new job reduce my intrinsic motivation enough that the gains in leadership, autonomy, impact, money, and future options do not compensate?”

If the OpenAI role is less interesting technically but gives you real agenda-setting power, strong mentorship experience, and future ability to shape research, it may still score high on autonomy/competence/meaning. If it is mostly management overhead on less compelling work, then the literature would predict a real satisfaction risk.

## Bottom line

The research broadly supports taking “interesting work” very seriously. Job content, autonomy, fit, meaning, and intrinsic motivation are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and are more strongly related to work happiness than marginal pay. Direct evidence on regret is limited, but adjacent research suggests people often regret not pursuing identity-relevant, meaningful paths.

For your specific choice, the leadership component is the complicating factor: it may be an intrinsically meaningful growth opportunity, not just an extrinsic reward. The best evidence-based comparison is therefore between the **day-to-day lived experience** of both jobs: autonomy, learning, meaning, people, stress, and how much of your time would be spent on work you actually find compelling.
