answer:
There isn’t a clean study of “stay in a fascinating frontier-AI research job vs. take a somewhat better-paid team-lead role on less interesting work.” But several adjacent literatures are pretty relevant. The short version is:

**Research generally finds that interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement, usually stronger than pay level once pay is adequate.** Salary still matters, especially for security, fairness, and life constraints, but marginal salary increases at already-high incomes tend to have modest well-being effects. The “leading a team” part should be treated not as an extrinsic perk, but as a potential source of autonomy, competence, impact, mentoring, status, stress, and loss of hands-on work.

## 1. Interesting work / job design is strongly related to satisfaction

A major framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating when they have skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback.

Relevant findings:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976** introduced the model.
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987** and **Loher et al., 1985** meta-analyzed job characteristics research and found that enriched jobs are reliably associated with higher job satisfaction and motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, a large meta-analysis of work design, found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, etc. — have moderate-to-large associations with job satisfaction, internal motivation, organizational commitment, and lower withdrawal.

The practical implication: “interesting work” is not a fluffy preference. In the research literature it maps onto several of the most robust predictors of job satisfaction: autonomy, learning, complexity, significance, and fit.

A related result: **person–job fit** matters a lot.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis: person–job fit was strongly associated with job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intention to quit. The corrected correlation with job satisfaction was roughly in the .5 range, which is large for organizational psychology.

So if your current role is unusually high-fit — intellectually, morally, socially, and in terms of your skills — that is a major variable.

## 2. Meaningful work predicts both job and general well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in research roles.

- **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997** found that people who experience their work as a “calling” report higher work and life satisfaction.
- **Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski, 2010** reviewed the meaningful-work literature and identified sources such as self-concordance, contribution, belonging, and authenticity.
- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work, found that meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, organizational commitment, and lower withdrawal intentions; it is also positively associated with broader life satisfaction and health.

This does not prove that “meaningful work causes happiness” in every case, because there is selection and personality confounding. But the association is one of the more consistent findings in work psychology.

## 3. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller than people expect

There is a very relevant meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”: pay level had only a modest relationship with job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction was more related, but still not huge. Their conclusion was basically that pay is less strongly related to job satisfaction than many people assume.

For general well-being:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that life evaluation rises with income, while emotional well-being appeared to plateau around $75k in 2010 dollars.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the plateau result, finding that experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly log-linearly.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled these somewhat: for many people well-being keeps rising with income, but for the least-happy subgroup there may be a flattening at higher incomes.

The most decision-relevant point: income effects are roughly **logarithmic**. Going from $80k to $160k is much bigger psychologically than going from $400k to $480k, even though both are +$80k. If both offers are already very high by normal standards, the marginal well-being effect of the salary delta is likely smaller than the effect of day-to-day work quality.

Also, people adapt to income and status changes. Pay matters more when it changes your constraints, security, housing, family options, or sense of being fairly treated. It matters less when it is just “somewhat more.”

## 4. Leadership could be intrinsically valuable, not merely extrinsic

The “leading a small team” piece is important. Research would not reduce that to “status” or “promotion.” It can affect several core psychological needs:

- **Autonomy**: more influence over direction.
- **Competence**: learning management, mentoring, strategy.
- **Relatedness**: helping others grow.
- **Impact**: coordinating larger efforts than you could do alone.
- **Status / recognition**: can feel good, but may be more adaptation-prone.
- **Stress / admin load**: meetings, conflict, performance management, less technical depth.

Self-determination theory is relevant here:

- **Gagné & Deci, 2005** and later SDT-at-work reviews argue that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central to motivation and well-being at work.
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014**, meta-analysis, found that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially for quality-sensitive work.

So the question is not “interesting work vs. management.” It is more like:

> Is team leadership itself an intrinsically meaningful and energizing activity for you, or is it mainly a credential/status/salary step?

If you have always wanted to lead because you want to mentor, set research taste, coordinate people, and have broader impact, then leadership may be a genuine intrinsic factor. If you want it mostly because it is the next rung, the research is less favorable.

## 5. People often overweight salary/status and underweight daily experience

Affective forecasting research is relevant.

- **Wilson & Gilbert, 2005** review evidence that people often mispredict the intensity and duration of future emotional reactions.
- **Kahneman et al., 2006**, “Would you be happier if you were richer? A focusing illusion,” argue that people overemphasize salient factors like income when predicting happiness.
- **Schkade & Kahneman, 1998** showed a similar “focusing illusion” in judgments about living in California: people overweight the salient difference and underweight ordinary daily life.

For career choices, this suggests caution: salary, title, and company brand are highly salient at decision time; the actual calendar you live through every week is less salient but often more important.

A useful research-consistent heuristic is: **imagine your average Tuesday six months after joining.** What fraction of the day is spent on work you find absorbing, meaningful, and energy-giving?

## 6. Regret research: career regrets are common, but not specifically “salary vs. interest”

There is less direct evidence on whether people regret choosing interesting work over higher salary. But regret research gives some hints.

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that people’s biggest life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and parenting. Career regrets are common.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that regrets of inaction tend to loom larger over time than regrets of action.

This cuts both ways. You might later regret leaving unusually interesting work. But you might also regret not taking a rare chance to lead if leadership is a central aspiration.

The regret literature does not say “always take the interesting job.” It says people regret missed opportunities, especially when the opportunity was aligned with a valued identity or goal.

## 7. Evidence-weighted interpretation for your situation

Based on the literature, I would weight the factors roughly like this:

1. **Day-to-day intellectual interest / meaningfulness / autonomy**: very important; strong predictor of job satisfaction.
2. **Fit with your values and identity**: very important.
3. **Opportunity to lead**: potentially very important if it is intrinsically motivating, not just status.
4. **Salary delta**: important if it changes life constraints, security, fairness, or family options; otherwise likely a weaker predictor.
5. **Prestige / brand / title**: psychologically salient, but often over-weighted and adaptation-prone.
6. **Reversibility / option value**: important but less studied directly; if one path keeps more future options open, that matters.

So the research-leaning answer is:

> If the salary increase is not life-changing and the OpenAI work is genuinely much less interesting, the literature would generally favor staying with the more interesting, meaningful, high-fit work.  
> But if leading a team is itself a deep intrinsic goal for you — mentoring, setting direction, scaling your impact — then the OpenAI role may not be “less interesting” in the relevant psychological sense; it may be interesting along a different dimension.

The key empirical question to ask yourself is not “Which job is objectively more interesting?” but:

> Which role gives me more of the job characteristics that robustly predict satisfaction: autonomy, competence growth, task significance, skill variety, feedback, social fit, and meaning?

## References to look up

- Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. 1976. *Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory.*
- Loher, B. T., Noe, R. A., Moeller, N. L., & Fitzgerald, M. P. 1985. *A meta-analysis of the relation of job characteristics to job satisfaction.*
- Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. 1987. *The validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A review and meta-analysis.*
- Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. 2007. *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.*
- Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. 2005. *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis.*
- Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. 2010. *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.*
- Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. 2010. *High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.*
- Killingsworth, M. A. 2021. *Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year.*
- Killingsworth, M. A., Kahneman, D., & Mellers, B. 2023. *Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved.*
- Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. 1997. *Jobs, careers, and callings.*
- Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. 2019. *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*
- Wilson, T. D., & Gilbert, D. T. 2005. *Affective forecasting.*
- Roese, N. J., & Summerville, A. 2005. *What we regret most... and why.*

answer:
Research doesn’t answer your exact “Anthropic vs OpenAI” case, but it has a fairly consistent answer to the broader question: **intrinsic features of the work — interestingness, autonomy, meaning, learning, fit with one’s abilities — are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement. Pay matters, but at already-high incomes its marginal effect is usually smaller unless the money changes your life circumstances, security, or status in a way you deeply care about.**

Below are the most relevant research findings.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is usually studied as intrinsic motivation / job design

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity — doing a whole meaningful piece of work  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback from the work itself

Meta-analyses generally find these features are **moderate-to-strong predictors of job satisfaction, motivation, and lower turnover intentions**.

Relevant papers:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976** — original Job Characteristics Model.  
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis — job characteristics predict job satisfaction and internal motivation.  
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis — motivational work characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback have substantial relationships with satisfaction and motivation.

A rough summary of that literature: correlations between intrinsic job characteristics and job satisfaction are often in the **~0.25–0.50** range, with autonomy and feedback often especially important. That is large by organizational psychology standards.

For your case, “interesting research” likely bundles several high-value job characteristics: skill variety, novelty, autonomy, task significance, learning, and a sense of progress.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

If a job fits what you are good at and what you want to do, people tend to be happier and less likely to leave.

A widely cited meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis on person–environment fit.

They found **person–job fit** was strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  
- lower intent to quit  
- performance, to a lesser degree

The correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction is often reported around **~0.50+**, which is quite high.

So if one role is much more aligned with your intrinsic interests and strengths, that is not a small consideration. The research would treat that as central.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect — especially past comfort/security

Pay is not irrelevant. But the relationship between pay and job satisfaction is usually modest.

A useful meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.”

They found that **actual pay level** has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .15** in many summaries. Pay satisfaction matters more than absolute pay, and perceived fairness/comparison effects matter a lot.

On broader life happiness:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found emotional well-being rose with income up to around $75k/year in 2008 dollars, while life evaluation continued to rise.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continues rising with income, roughly logarithmically, with no sharp plateau.  
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled these: income keeps helping for many people, but for the least happy group it plateaus after a point; effects are real but not huge.

At already-high tech/AI salaries, the key question is usually: **is the salary difference life-changing or just nice?** A 10–30% increase may matter less for daily happiness than the daily content of your work, unless it changes housing, family plans, financial independence timeline, perceived fairness, etc.

---

## 4. Meaningful work has strong associations with satisfaction and well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research careers they often overlap.

Relevant literature:

- **Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski, 2010** — review of meaningful work.  
- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work.

Meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- engagement  
- organizational commitment  
- lower withdrawal intentions  
- somewhat higher general life satisfaction

Correlations with work outcomes are often in the **~0.40–0.60** range. Effects on overall life satisfaction are smaller but still meaningful.

One caveat: the “calling” literature shows that people who experience work as a calling are often more satisfied, but they may also tolerate worse pay or conditions.

- **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997** — job/career/calling orientations.  
- **Bunderson & Thompson, 2009** — zookeepers saw work as a calling and accepted sacrifices; meaningful work can make people vulnerable to exploitation.

So “interesting work” is valuable, but not infinitely valuable.

---

## 5. Boring work is reliably bad for satisfaction

There is also research on **workplace boredom**. It generally finds that boredom is associated with:

- lower job satisfaction  
- lower engagement  
- higher stress/burnout symptoms  
- higher turnover intention  
- sometimes counterproductive behavior

This matters because “less interesting” can become “boring” or “deadening” if the daily work does not use your core abilities. For highly intrinsically motivated research people, this can be a large downside.

---

## 6. Job changes often produce a “honeymoon,” then adaptation

There is longitudinal evidence that after changing jobs people often experience a temporary boost in satisfaction, followed by decline as novelty wears off.

Relevant paper:

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005**, “The relationship between employee job change and job satisfaction: The honeymoon-hangover effect.”

This is relevant to offers involving higher salary, title, prestige, or a new role. Those can feel very compelling ex ante, but some benefits adapt away. Daily task content, autonomy, workload, and fit may remain more persistently important.

---

## 7. Leadership/team management is a separate intrinsic factor, not just “less interesting”

You mentioned that leading a small team is something you always wanted to do. Research would not treat that as merely extrinsic. Leadership can provide:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- status  
- responsibility  
- broader impact  
- learning

Those are also psychologically important. But management can also bring:

- more meetings  
- less deep work  
- more people problems  
- less direct research ownership  
- higher ambiguity  
- emotional labor  
- responsibility without full control

So the question is not simply “interesting work vs salary.” It may be:

> “Do I want to optimize for deep technical/research interest, or for growth into leadership and broader organizational impact?”

Both can be intrinsically meaningful. The risk is taking a role where the *title* is attractive but the daily work is not.

---

## 8. Regret: evidence is less direct

There is not much clean research saying “people who chose interesting work over higher salary later regretted it less.” Career regret research is messier.

General regret findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** — people often regret actions in the short term, but inactions over the long term.  
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** — education and career are among the most common life regret domains, often involving missed opportunities.

This cuts both ways for you:

- You might regret leaving uniquely interesting research for a less interesting role.  
- You might also regret not trying leadership if it is a long-standing goal and the opportunity is scarce.

So the regret literature does not give a simple “stay with the interesting job” answer. It suggests you should pay attention to **which future inaction would haunt you more**: not doing the most interesting research, or not taking a leadership path when offered.

---

## My research-based synthesis

If the salary difference is not life-changing, the literature would generally put more weight on:

1. person–job fit  
2. autonomy  
3. meaningful/interesting daily work  
4. learning and skill use  
5. quality of coworkers/manager  
6. workload/stress  
7. only then marginal pay

But if leading a team is a genuine intrinsic goal for you, it belongs in the same category as “interesting work,” not in the salary/status bucket.

A useful decision question would be:

> “Ignoring salary and logo, which job gives me better daily work over the next two years: more autonomy, more learning, more meaningful progress, and better fit with the person I want to become?”

If OpenAI gives you real leadership, mentorship, ownership, and growth, the “less interesting” research might be worth it. If it mainly gives higher pay and a management title while pulling you away from the work you find alive, research would predict a meaningful risk of lower satisfaction after the initial honeymoon.

answer:
Short version: yes—there is a fairly large literature, and it generally says that **intrinsic work quality**—interesting/challenging work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, fit with your interests/values—is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement. **Pay matters**, but once pay is high enough and perceived as fair, its marginal association with happiness/job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect. **Leadership/management** can be positive if it gives autonomy, impact, growth, and social meaning; it can be negative if it mainly adds coordination burden, politics, and loss of craft work.

Direct studies of “people who turned down a more lucrative but less interesting job—did they later regret it?” are rare. But related evidence is pretty informative.

## 1. “Interesting work” is not a luxury variable; it is central in job-satisfaction research

Organizational psychology often operationalizes “interesting work” as some combination of:

- autonomy/control over work,
- skill variety,
- task significance/meaning,
- task identity/ownership,
- feedback/learning,
- person–job fit,
- intrinsic motivation.

The classic Job Characteristics Model—Hackman & Oldham—and later meta-analyses find that these factors predict job satisfaction, motivation, and lower burnout/turnover intentions.

A representative meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, meta-analysis of work design: motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, and skill variety are consistently associated with higher job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower strain. Autonomy is especially robust.

Another strong line of evidence:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, meta-analysis on person–job/person–organization fit: person–job fit correlates strongly with job satisfaction—roughly around the .5 range in corrected correlations—and is associated with organizational commitment and lower intent to quit.  
  Translation: when the work matches the person’s interests/abilities/values, people are substantially more satisfied and less likely to want out.

Also relevant:

- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012** and related vocational-interest meta-analyses: interest congruence predicts satisfaction, performance, and persistence, though effect sizes vary by occupation and measure.

So if by “interesting” you mean “this work uses my best abilities, I care about the problems, I have autonomy, I learn, and the work feels meaningful,” then the research treats that as a major determinant of job satisfaction—not a minor perk.

## 2. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually modest—especially at high incomes

There are two related literatures: job-satisfaction studies and general subjective-well-being/income studies.

On job satisfaction:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: actual pay level has a positive but modest relationship with job satisfaction—around **r ≈ .15** with overall job satisfaction and somewhat higher with pay satisfaction.  
  Translation: being paid more helps, but pay explains much less variation in job satisfaction than job content, fit, autonomy, and relationships.

On general happiness/income:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation continued rising with income.
- **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continues rising with income more smoothly than the original “plateau” story suggested.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers 2023** reconciled these: for many people, well-being keeps increasing with log income, but for the least-happy subgroup it may flatten after a threshold.

The practical implication is not “money doesn’t matter.” It is: **income has diminishing marginal returns**, especially if you are already highly paid. A “bit higher salary” may matter a lot if it changes your financial security, family options, runway, or perceived fairness. But if it is a modest percentage increase on an already high compensation base, research would predict a smaller happiness effect than a large change in day-to-day work quality.

## 3. Meaningful work is especially predictive of satisfaction and engagement

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research-heavy jobs they often overlap.

Meta-analytic work on meaningful work finds strong associations with positive job outcomes:

- **Allan et al. 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work: meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower turnover intentions.

There is also evidence from scientists specifically:

- **Stern 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** found that scientists accept lower wages for jobs that allow more scientific openness/publication. The estimated wage discount was large—on the order of 20% in that setting.  
  This does not prove they were happier, but it is revealed-preference evidence that technical/scientific workers often trade money for intrinsic research attributes.

For someone in AI research, this literature is unusually relevant: highly skilled researchers often have strong “taste for science/ideas,” and those preferences can dominate moderate pay differences.

## 4. Leadership can be intrinsically rewarding—but management is not automatically happier

Your OpenAI offer includes leading a small team, which is not merely an extrinsic factor. It could satisfy several intrinsic motives:

- autonomy,
- influence,
- mentoring/coaching,
- status/responsibility,
- larger impact,
- career growth,
- identity as a leader.

Research would not classify that as “just title.” It can be a real source of motivation.

However, promotion/leadership evidence is mixed. Promotions often increase pay, status, and satisfaction, but can also increase stress and reduce time spent on the work one originally enjoyed.

Relevant findings:

- Studies of promotions often find positive effects on job satisfaction, but not always on health or stress.
- **Boyce & Oswald 2012** found that promotion did not necessarily improve health and was associated with greater mental strain in some data.
- Job demands–resources research—e.g. **Bakker & Demerouti 2007**—suggests that responsibility is good when paired with autonomy, support, and resources; it becomes harmful when it creates overload, ambiguity, or politics.

So the research-backed question is not “is leading a team good?” but:

> Will this leadership role give you more autonomy, learning, impact, and meaningful social contribution—or will it mainly replace intrinsically interesting research with coordination, performance management, and organizational overhead?

That distinction matters a lot.

## 5. People often overweight salient, comparable features like salary/title

There is relevant decision-psychology evidence here.

When comparing two offers side by side, people tend to overweight features that are easy to compare numerically—salary, level, team size, title—and underweight features that will determine everyday experience. This is related to:

- **Hsee & Zhang’s “distinction bias”**: in joint evaluation, people overemphasize quantitative differences that may matter less in actual lived experience.
- **Kahneman’s “focusing illusion”**: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”
- Affective forecasting work by **Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman** and others: people often mispredict the duration/intensity of happiness from major changes.

This does not mean “ignore salary/title.” It means: do not decide only from the offer-sheet comparison. Forecast the actual Tuesday/Wednesday experience six months in.

## 6. Regret evidence is indirect, but fit/satisfaction predicts quitting

Direct regret studies on this exact tradeoff are limited. But turnover research is relevant:

- **Griffeth, Hom & Gaertner 2000**, meta-analysis: job satisfaction and organizational commitment predict turnover/intentions to quit.
- Person–job fit and meaningful work also predict lower turnover intentions.

So if a move reduces your intrinsic fit substantially, the empirical expectation is higher risk of dissatisfaction and wanting to leave. Conversely, if the leadership role creates a new kind of fit—mentoring, leverage, ownership—it could be satisfying despite less direct research interest.

## 7. What the evidence implies for your specific tradeoff

On average, the literature would say:

1. **“Much less interesting work” is a serious negative**, not a small aesthetic preference.
2. **A modest salary increase is probably a weaker predictor of happiness**, especially if your current compensation is already high and financial constraints are not binding.
3. **Leading a small team could be a major positive** if it is genuinely the kind of work you want—coaching, setting direction, owning outcomes—not just status.
4. The biggest unknown is the actual day-to-day composition of the new role.

A research-backed way to evaluate would be to estimate, for each option:

- percentage of week spent doing work you find intrinsically absorbing;
- autonomy over research/technical direction;
- quality of manager/leadership chain;
- expected meeting/coordination load;
- degree of task significance/mission alignment;
- learning curve and skill growth;
- likelihood that the role still feels attractive after the novelty of title/salary wears off.

If the OpenAI role is “less interesting topic, but more autonomy, leadership, impact, and growth,” the evidence could support taking it. If it is “less interesting topic, modest pay bump, and lots more coordination/admin,” the evidence would generally favor staying with the more intrinsically interesting role.

The cleanest summary from the research is:

> People tend to be happier in jobs that fit their interests, values, and preferred mode of working. Pay matters, but moderate pay differences are usually less predictive of sustained job satisfaction than autonomy, meaning, fit, and the nature of the work itself.

answer:
Yes—there is a lot of relevant research, though not usually phrased as “interesting work vs. slightly higher pay.” The closest literatures are **job design**, **intrinsic motivation**, **person-job fit / interest fit**, **meaningful work**, **pay and happiness**, and **career regret**.

Short version: **work that is intrinsically interesting, autonomy-supportive, skill-using, and meaningful is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and often spills over into life satisfaction. Pay matters, but once basic financial needs and fairness are met, the marginal effect of “somewhat more money” is usually much smaller than the effect of work content, autonomy, fit, and meaning.** Leadership can be a positive factor too, but mostly insofar as it gives autonomy, growth, status, influence, and relationship quality—not merely because it is “management.”

## 1. Job design research: interesting work strongly predicts satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980. It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

These map pretty closely onto “interesting research work”: complex problem-solving, autonomy, intellectual challenge, visible impact, etc.

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** found that motivational job characteristics are strongly related to outcomes like job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. In particular, things like **autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and job complexity** have moderate-to-large correlations with job satisfaction.

Approximate pattern from that literature:

- Autonomy: strongly associated with job satisfaction and motivation  
- Skill variety / complexity / problem-solving: positively associated with satisfaction  
- Task significance / meaningfulness: positively associated with satisfaction  
- Feedback: positively associated with satisfaction  
- These also predict lower intent to quit  

So from the job-design literature, “this work is intellectually alive and intrinsically engaging” is not a minor perk; it is one of the central determinants of job satisfaction.

## 2. Person-job fit and interest fit: fit matters a lot

Another relevant literature is **person-environment fit**, especially **person-job fit** and **vocational interest congruence**.

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person-job fit is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  
- lower intent to quit  
- performance, though more modestly  

The correlation between person-job fit and job satisfaction is often quite large by social science standards.

There is also vocational-interest research from the Holland/RIASEC tradition. Meta-analyses such as **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012** find that interest fit predicts job performance, satisfaction, and persistence. People tend to do better and stay longer when their work matches their interests.

For your case, the relevant question is not just “is OpenAI or Anthropic better?” but: **which role better fits the kind of work you reliably find absorbing?** If your strongest fit is with frontier research/engineering problems, a less interesting management-heavy role may create lower person-job fit even if it has more status and compensation.

## 3. Meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job attitudes

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but in research roles they often overlap: work can feel meaningful because the problems are important, intellectually deep, and connected to a mission.

A meta-analysis by **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay 2019** found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- life satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  
- lower withdrawal / turnover intentions  

The associations with job satisfaction and engagement are large. Meaningful work is one of the more robust positive predictors in the organizational-psychology literature.

A caveat: “meaningful work” can also make people tolerate overwork or exploitation. There is research on “calling” showing both benefits and risks. For example, people with a strong calling often report more meaning and satisfaction, but may also accept worse conditions or have difficulty detaching. So meaningful/interesting work is powerful, but it is not automatically healthy if paired with chronic stress, poor boundaries, or low control.

## 4. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases usually have smaller effects than work quality

The best-known meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**. They found that pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is relatively modest. Roughly speaking, pay has a small-to-moderate association with pay satisfaction and a smaller association with overall job satisfaction.

This does not mean money is unimportant. It matters a lot when it affects security, fairness, family options, housing, debt, freedom, etc. But if you are already well compensated and the difference is “a bit higher salary,” the expected happiness effect is usually limited.

On subjective well-being more generally:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that life evaluation rises with income, while emotional well-being showed diminishing returns around higher income levels in their data.  
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the sharp plateau result, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, though roughly logarithmically.  
- A later adversarial collaboration, **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023**, suggested a more nuanced picture: happiness tends to keep increasing with income for many people, but the shape differs across individuals.

The important practical point from this literature: **income has diminishing marginal utility**. A 10–20% raise can matter, especially if it changes constraints or future freedom, but it usually does not compensate indefinitely for a large drop in daily work quality, autonomy, interest, or meaning.

## 5. Intrinsic motivation tends to predict engagement and well-being

Self-Determination Theory, associated with **Deci & Ryan**, is another relevant framework. It argues that motivation and well-being depend heavily on satisfaction of three needs:

- autonomy  
- competence  
- relatedness  

Work that is interesting often satisfies autonomy and competence: “I choose difficult problems and get better at them.” Leading a team may satisfy competence, relatedness, autonomy, and status—but could also reduce autonomy if the role is more coordination, hiring, performance management, or organizational politics than deep work.

Meta-analytic work, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, finds that satisfaction of these psychological needs at work is strongly related to job satisfaction, engagement, well-being, and lower burnout.

A related finding: extrinsic rewards are not bad, but for already-interesting tasks, strongly controlling extrinsic incentives can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation. This comes from the **Deci, Koestner & Ryan 1999** meta-analysis and later work. The more general and less controversial takeaway is: **intrinsic motivation and autonomy are not interchangeable with pay.**

## 6. People often mispredict how much salary/status changes will affect happiness

Affective forecasting research by **Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman** and others shows that people often overestimate how much major life changes will affect their long-term happiness. This is especially true when focusing on salient, easily compared variables: salary, title, prestige, status.

Kahneman has called this the **focusing illusion**: “Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it.”

This does not mean salary or title do not matter. But it suggests caution: when choosing between jobs, people may overweight the visible comparison — “higher salary, lead title” — and underweight what their daily experience will be like six months later.

Daily work content is repeated exposure. If the work is boring or frustrating every day, that is not a one-time cost. Conversely, if the work is consistently engaging, that is a repeated benefit.

## 7. Regret research: career regrets are common, but the evidence is less specific

There is less direct research on “people who left interesting jobs for higher pay later regretted it” versus the reverse. But general regret research has some relevant patterns.

**Roese & Summerville 2005** found that people’s most common regrets often involve education, career, romance, and parenting. Career is one of the big regret domains.

Another common finding, associated with **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**, is that regrets of **inaction** tend to grow over time, while regrets of action are often more painful in the short run but fade more. This might cut both ways in your situation:

- If you turn down a team-lead opportunity you always wanted, you may later wonder “what if I had tried leadership?”  
- If you leave unusually interesting research, you may later regret giving up rare work fit.  

The regret literature does not give a clean answer here. It mostly says that people regret missed opportunities, especially in domains central to identity. So the important thing is to identify which opportunity is more identity-central for you: **frontier research depth** or **becoming a leader/manager**.

## 8. Leadership specifically: potentially valuable, but depends on the actual work

The research does not say “management makes people happier” in a simple way. Leadership roles can increase:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- status  
- pay  
- social impact  
- learning  
- mentoring opportunities  

All of those can improve satisfaction.

But leadership can also increase:

- meetings  
- interruptions  
- responsibility without control  
- conflict management  
- performance evaluation burden  
- politics  
- reduced time for deep technical work  

From a job-design perspective, the key question is whether the team-lead role increases or decreases your core need satisfaction: autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaningfulness, and task interest.

A “small team lead doing still-interesting technical work” is very different from “mostly management on less interesting projects.”

## 9. How I’d summarize the research for your decision

Based on the evidence, the strongest predictors of long-term work satisfaction are likely to be:

1. **Person-job fit** — does the role match what you are naturally drawn to?  
2. **Autonomy** — will you have real control over your work?  
3. **Interesting/challenging work** — will the day-to-day problems absorb you?  
4. **Meaning/significance** — do you care about the mission and outputs?  
5. **Competence/growth** — will you keep getting better at something you value?  
6. **Relatedness/team quality** — will you like and respect the people?  
7. **Fairness and sufficient pay** — are compensation and status good enough not to become a resentment?  
8. **Workload/stress sustainability** — is the job energizing or depleting?  

A modest salary increase by itself is usually not a major predictor of happiness once you are already well paid. But a leadership opportunity could matter a lot if it satisfies a deep long-term preference. The question is whether it is genuinely a path toward the kind of work-life you want, or just an attractive external marker.

The research-backed version of the decision might be:

> Prefer the role with better expected day-to-day fit, autonomy, meaningfulness, growth, and sustainable challenge, unless the pay/leadership difference materially changes your life options or is central to your long-term identity.

In your specific framing — “higher salary and leading a small team, but much less interesting” — the literature would generally warn against underestimating the cost of “much less interesting.” That factor is strongly tied to job satisfaction and engagement. But it would also warn against ignoring a rare leadership opportunity if leading people is a core aspiration rather than just a title/pay upgrade.

answer:
Short version: the research literature is fairly consistent that **day-to-day work content and fit — interesting tasks, autonomy, learning, meaning, good collaborators — are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction**. **Pay matters**, but among already-high earners the marginal effect of “a bit more salary” is usually much smaller than differences in work quality. The tricky part in your case is that “leading a small team” is not merely extrinsic; it may satisfy autonomy, mastery, impact, identity, and long-term growth — so the relevant comparison is not “interesting work vs money,” but **interesting research work vs a possibly less intrinsically interesting but more developmental/leadership-shaped role**.

Below are the research results I’d put most weight on.

---

## 1. Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work is strongly tied to job satisfaction

A large body of organizational psychology finds that certain job characteristics reliably predict satisfaction, engagement, motivation, and lower turnover intentions:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through**
- **Feedback**
- **Learning/challenge**

This comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** of Hackman & Oldham and many later meta-analyses.

Notable results:

- **Fried & Ferris 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model: jobs high in autonomy, skill variety, significance, etc. are associated with higher job satisfaction and internal motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, large meta-analysis of work-design research: motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, complexity — had fairly strong relationships with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and lower stress/turnover intentions. Correlations are often in the roughly **.3–.5** range, which is large by social-science standards.

So, in research terms, “interesting work” is not a fluffy variable. It maps onto well-studied predictors: **intrinsic motivation, skill use, challenge, autonomy, and meaning**.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

If by “interesting” you mean “this work fits my abilities, values, curiosity, and preferred problems,” then the relevant literature is **person–job fit** and **vocational interest congruence**.

Findings:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, meta-analysis: person–job fit had a strong association with job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .56** — and was also related to organizational commitment and lower intent to quit.
- Meta-analyses on **vocational interest congruence** — e.g. Assouline & Meir 1987; later updates — find that people whose jobs match their interests tend to be more satisfied and stay longer, although effect sizes are more modest than broad person–job fit.

This is probably one of the most relevant findings for your situation. If your current role is unusually well-matched to what you find intellectually alive, the literature would treat that as a major contributor to long-term satisfaction.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is often smaller than people expect — especially at high income

There is also a large literature on income and well-being.

Relevant results:

- **Judge et al. 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: actual pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest — roughly **r ≈ .15** with job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay.
- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising with income.
- **Killingsworth 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023**: newer results suggest well-being continues to rise with income more than Kahneman & Deaton originally found, but roughly with **log income** — meaning each additional dollar matters less as income rises.

For a high-earning research engineer, the implication is: a salary increase can matter, especially for savings, family plans, security, or perceived fairness. But if the difference is “a bit higher salary,” research would generally predict its direct happiness effect to be **smaller than a large change in work fit, autonomy, or daily interest**.

A useful heuristic from the income literature: a move from, say, \$100k to \$150k may matter more than \$500k to \$600k, because subjective effects scale roughly with **percentage/log changes**, not absolute dollars.

---

## 4. Intrinsic goals predict well-being better than extrinsic goals

Self-determination theory is relevant here. It distinguishes between goals satisfying:

- **Autonomy**
- **Competence**
- **Relatedness**
- **Meaning/growth**

versus more extrinsic goals such as money, image, and status.

Findings:

- **Kasser & Ryan 1993/1996**: stronger emphasis on extrinsic aspirations such as wealth, fame, and image is associated with lower well-being, controlling for other factors.
- **Niemiec, Ryan & Deci 2009**, longitudinal study of college graduates: attaining intrinsic goals was associated with increased well-being; attaining extrinsic goals was not similarly beneficial and in some cases related to ill-being.
- **Deci, Koestner & Ryan 1999**, meta-analysis: external rewards can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when experienced as controlling. This is debated in details, but the broader point is robust: externally-driven motivation is less reliably satisfying than autonomy/competence/meaning-driven motivation.

But an important caveat: **leading a team is not necessarily extrinsic**. If leadership is something you “always wanted to do,” it may be an intrinsic growth goal — competence, agency, mentorship, impact. In that case, the research does not say “stay with the more intellectually interesting IC role.” It says: choose the role that better satisfies your deeper psychological needs.

---

## 5. Management/leadership can increase satisfaction — or reduce it — depending on autonomy vs demands

The literature on job demands and resources is useful here.

- **Job Demands–Resources model**, e.g. Bakker & Demerouti: job resources such as autonomy, learning, social support, and impact predict engagement; high demands predict strain and burnout, especially without resources.
- Management roles often provide more **control, influence, status, and impact**.
- But they also add **meetings, conflict resolution, hiring/performance management, ambiguity, emotional labor, and less maker time**.

So “leading a small team” could be a major positive if it gives you:

- more autonomy,
- more leverage,
- more mentoring,
- more ownership,
- a path toward the identity you want.

But it could be negative if it mostly means:

- less research depth,
- more coordination overhead,
- more politics,
- less time in flow,
- responsibility without real authority.

The research does not support a blanket “management makes people happier.” It supports: **higher autonomy and meaningful responsibility are good; high demands and low fit are bad.**

---

## 6. Direct research on “regretting choosing the interesting job” is thinner

There is less direct evidence on career regret specifically of the form: “people who chose interesting work over money later regret it.” But adjacent literatures are suggestive.

Relevant findings:

- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: over longer time horizons, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.
- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: career and education are among the most common domains of regret.
- **Davidai & Gilovich 2018**: people’s most enduring regrets often involve failing to pursue their “ideal self” — the person they wanted to become — more than failing to meet obligations.
- **Whillans et al. 2016/2017**: people who value time over money tend to report higher happiness; spending money to save time can improve well-being.

Implication for you: regret may attach not only to leaving interesting work, but also to **not trying leadership** if that is part of your ideal future self. The literature would ask: which path feels more like an “ideal-self” aspiration versus external validation?

---

## 7. “Interesting” also relates to psychological richness, not just happiness

Recent work by **Shigehiro Oishi and colleagues** distinguishes three good-life dimensions:

1. Happiness — comfort, pleasure, stability.
2. Meaning — purpose, contribution, coherence.
3. Psychological richness — novelty, complexity, interesting experiences, perspective change.

People differ in which they value. Some studies find that a nontrivial minority would choose a psychologically rich life even over a merely happy or meaningful one.

This is relevant because research work at the frontier may provide psychological richness: novelty, intellectual challenge, deep problems. A less interesting but higher-status role may be better on some dimensions and worse on this one.

---

## Evidence-weighted takeaway

If you strip away personal details, the research suggests:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, well-fitting work is a major predictor of job satisfaction and well-being.**
2. **A modest salary increase is usually a weaker predictor, especially once you already earn enough for security and freedom.**
3. **Leadership could matter a lot if it is intrinsically important to you**, not merely because it is higher status.
4. **The biggest risk of moving is losing daily intrinsic motivation.**
5. **The biggest risk of staying is failing to pursue a valued leadership/developmental path**, which could become a regret if it is central to your desired identity.

So the research does not imply “always stay in the more interesting job.” It implies something closer to:

> Prefer the option with the best combination of intrinsic interest, autonomy, meaning, skill growth, and person–job fit. Treat modest pay differences as secondary unless they materially change your life. Treat leadership as valuable if it genuinely satisfies growth, autonomy, and impact — not merely title/status.

A practical research-aligned question would be:

> In two years, which role is more likely to give me high autonomy, deep learning, meaningful impact, good collaborators, and work I look forward to doing most days?

If the OpenAI role is “less interesting research but much more autonomy, mentorship, and long-term identity fit,” it may be a good move. If it is “less interesting work plus a title and slightly more money,” the literature would generally favor staying with the more interesting, better-fit role.

answer:
There isn’t much research on the exact question “should a research engineer take a less interesting but higher-paid/team-lead role?” But several literatures are relevant. The short version is:

> Across organizational psychology, the “work itself” — autonomy, skill use, task significance, learning, fit with interests/values, and meaningfulness — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work engagement. Pay matters, but actual pay level has a surprisingly modest association with overall job satisfaction once basic needs/fairness are met. Evidence on regret is thinner, but long-term career regrets often involve foregone opportunities, especially “I never tried X,” so a long-desired leadership opportunity is not trivial either.

## 1. Interesting/meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction

A large body of research comes from the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham, later meta-analyzed by Fried & Ferris and by Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson.

Core job features that predict satisfaction and motivation include:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task identity** — seeing a whole piece of work through
- **Task significance** — the work matters
- **Feedback from the work itself**
- **Complexity/learning opportunities**

In the meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, motivational job characteristics had substantial relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions. Individual correlations vary, but many are in the roughly **r = .30–.50** range. Social support and good interpersonal context are similarly important.

Interpretation: if “interesting work” means “I get to use my best skills on hard, meaningful problems with autonomy,” the literature treats that as a central driver of satisfaction, not a luxury.

## 2. Person–job fit is a very large predictor

Another relevant area is **person–environment fit** or **person–job fit**. This asks: does the job match your values, abilities, interests, and desired way of working?

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** found that person–job fit was strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. The corrected correlation between person–job fit and job satisfaction was around **r ≈ .56**, which is large for this kind of research.

So if your current Anthropic role is unusually well-matched to your intellectual interests, that is not a small factor. Conversely, if the OpenAI role matches another deep preference — e.g. “I want to lead and mentor a team” — that also counts as fit.

## 3. Meaningful work predicts both job and life satisfaction

There is also a literature on **meaningful work**. A meta-analysis by **Allan et al. 2019** found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- Job satisfaction — roughly large correlation, often around **r ≈ .60**
- Work engagement
- Organizational commitment
- Lower withdrawal intentions
- Lower burnout
- Life satisfaction — smaller but still meaningful, roughly **r ≈ .30–.40**

This is relevant if “interesting” also means “important,” “mission-aligned,” or “I’m proud of the work.” Meaningful work tends to spill over into general well-being, not just workplace attitudes.

## 4. Pay matters, but actual pay level is usually a weaker predictor of job satisfaction

The most directly relevant salary result is from:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

They found that **actual pay level** had only a modest association with overall job satisfaction — about **r ≈ .15**. Pay level correlated somewhat more with pay satisfaction, but still not hugely. Pay satisfaction itself relates more to overall job satisfaction than objective pay does, because fairness, comparison group, expectations, and needs matter.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. Money matters more when it changes:

- Financial security
- Housing/family constraints
- Ability to buy time
- Perceived fairness/status
- Freedom to take risks later
- Ability to donate/invest/save, if those are important values

But if both options already clear your financial threshold, the marginal happiness effect of “somewhat higher salary” is often smaller than people intuitively expect.

Related subjective well-being research also suggests that income has diminishing marginal returns, often approximately logarithmic. **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found a plateau for emotional well-being around a certain income level, though later work by **Killingsworth 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** revised this: well-being continues to rise for many people, but not equally, and less for the least happy group after a threshold. The practical point is still: salary can matter, but its marginal effect is usually much smaller than day-to-day work experience once you are already well-paid.

## 5. Job satisfaction strongly affects overall life satisfaction

This matters because people sometimes treat job satisfaction as “just work.” But work is a large fraction of waking life.

Meta-analyses such as:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989**
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang 2010**

find a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .40–.45**. The causality likely runs both ways: happier people are more satisfied with jobs, but better jobs also improve well-being.

So choosing between jobs is not just a career optimization problem; it likely affects broader well-being.

## 6. Intrinsic motivation matters especially for creative/research work

For research or engineering work involving ambiguity and creativity, intrinsic interest may be especially important.

Relevant findings:

- **Amabile’s research on creativity** argues that intrinsic motivation is a major ingredient of creative performance.
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford 2014**, a meta-analysis, found that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially for complex or quality-focused tasks, while extrinsic incentives are more predictive of quantity-type outcomes.

For frontier AI research/engineering, the work is likely closer to “complex, creative, quality-sensitive” than to routine production. So the performance cost of “less interesting” may be nontrivial, not just the happiness cost.

## 7. Leadership could be a genuine intrinsic motivator, not merely extrinsic status

The OpenAI offer has two components: higher salary and leading a small team. The leadership part should not be lumped in with salary. Research would treat it as potentially meaningful because it can increase:

- Autonomy/control
- Task significance
- Identity/status
- Learning/growth
- Relatedness/mentoring
- Influence over direction

Under the **Job Demands–Resources** model, leadership can be both a resource and a demand. It may improve engagement if it gives real authority, support, and meaningful responsibility. It may reduce well-being if it mostly adds coordination load, conflict, accountability without control, or less time for the work you intrinsically value.

So the research-relevant question is not “IC interesting work vs extrinsic management.” It is more like:

> Does leading this team satisfy a deep preference for growth, agency, mentorship, and influence, or is it mostly a title plus admin burden attached to less interesting work?

That distinction matters a lot.

## 8. Regret evidence is indirect, but career regrets are common

There is much less direct evidence on “do people regret choosing the interesting job over the higher-paying one?”

But regret research gives some clues:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that education, career, and romance are among the most common life-regret domains. Career regrets are very common.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** and related work found that in the short term people often regret actions, but in the long term people more often regret **inactions** — things they did not try.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- If the OpenAI leadership role is a rare chance to try something you have “always wanted,” passing on it could become a salient “I never tried leading” regret.
- If Anthropic currently gives you unusually meaningful, high-interest work, leaving it for a less interesting role could become “I gave up the work I actually cared about for salary/title.”

The regret literature does not say which one dominates. It says foregone opportunities loom large, especially when they remain psychologically open: “I could have done that.”

## 9. A research-based way to frame the decision

Based on the literature, I would separate the factors like this:

### Factors with strong evidence for well-being/job satisfaction

- Daily intellectual interest
- Autonomy
- Learning/growth
- Meaning/task significance
- Person–job fit
- Quality of manager/team/social environment
- Workload and burnout risk
- Ability to use your strongest skills

### Factors that matter but often less than expected

- Marginal salary increase, if already financially comfortable
- Prestige/status, unless it strongly affects identity, options, or values
- Title, unless it changes actual autonomy/influence

### Factors that may matter a lot for regret/long-term development

- Trying leadership if it is a deep, persistent goal
- Reversibility: can you return to research IC work later?
- Option value: which choice opens more future paths?
- Scarcity: which opportunity is rarer for you?
- Whether the new role gives real leadership or just management load

## Bottom-line synthesis from the research

If the salary difference is modest relative to your current financial situation, the research generally suggests not overweighting it. Intrinsic work quality, autonomy, meaning, and person–job fit are usually stronger predictors of job satisfaction than actual pay level.

But the leadership component is different. If leading a small team is a deeply held goal and the role gives genuine autonomy, mentorship, and strategic influence, then it may itself be a form of meaningful/interesting work — even if the technical problem is less interesting.

So the evidence does **not** say “always choose the more interesting job.” It says something closer to:

> Choose the role with the best expected day-to-day fit across autonomy, meaning, interest, growth, social environment, and sustainability. Treat salary as important mainly insofar as it changes security/options. Treat leadership as important if it is a real source of intrinsic motivation and future fit, not just an external marker.

**Evaluating job enrichment**

answer:
Short version: yes, there is a lot of evidence that “interesting / meaningful / autonomy-rich work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction, engagement, and general well-being. The marginal effect of higher pay is real but usually much smaller, especially once someone is already financially comfortable. However, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk: leadership can increase autonomy, responsibility, learning, impact, and identity fit, all of which are also among the strongest predictors of satisfaction. The research does **not** cleanly say “always choose the more interesting IC work”; it says to look carefully at the actual day-to-day job design.

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction

The closest research tradition is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham, later meta-analyzed by Fried & Ferris, Humphrey et al., etc. It focuses on things like:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  
- job complexity / problem solving  
- opportunities to use and develop abilities  

Meta-analyses generally find these characteristics have moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal intentions. In Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson’s 2007 meta-analysis, job autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, and social support all had substantial correlations with satisfaction and motivation, often in the rough `.3–.5` range.

For a research engineer, “interesting work” probably maps to several of these at once: complexity, skill use, learning, autonomy, novelty, and task significance. So if your current role is genuinely high on those, the literature would predict that it is a real source of well-being, not just a nice-to-have.

## 2. Meaningful work also strongly predicts satisfaction

Another nearby literature is **meaningful work**. Meta-analytic work, e.g. Allan et al. 2019, finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- engagement  
- organizational commitment  
- lower turnover intentions  
- somewhat higher life satisfaction  

The correlations with job satisfaction and engagement are often large by organizational psychology standards. Meaningful work is not identical to “interesting work,” but in research roles they often overlap: “I care about the problem,” “I think this matters,” “I am proud of the work,” “I am using my abilities on something important.”

If Anthropic feels more meaningful or more intellectually alive to you, that is a meaningful empirical input.

## 3. Person–job fit and interest congruence matter

There is also research on **person–job fit** and **vocational interest congruence**.

Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 meta-analyzed person–environment fit and found that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. Interest-congruence studies, including work by Spokane, Meir, Nye, Su, Rounds and others, usually find smaller but reliable effects: people whose work matches their interests tend to be more satisfied and more persistent.

The effects are not deterministic. Plenty of people are happy in jobs that are not their “perfect interest match,” because social environment, autonomy, pay, status, family constraints, and identity also matter. But the evidence is quite consistent that fit with interests and abilities is beneficial.

## 4. Pay matters, but the marginal effect is usually smaller

Pay absolutely matters, especially when it changes security, freedom, family options, location, retirement timeline, or perceived fairness. But at the individual level, the relationship between pay level and overall job satisfaction is usually modest.

A commonly cited meta-analysis is Judge et al. 2010, which found that individual pay level had only a small-to-moderate relationship with pay satisfaction and a relatively small relationship with overall job satisfaction — roughly around `r ≈ .15` for job satisfaction, depending on specification. In plain terms: higher-paid people are somewhat more satisfied, but pay explains much less variance than job design, fit, autonomy, social support, and meaning.

On general well-being, the income literature is nuanced. Kahneman & Deaton 2010 found that life evaluation keeps rising with income but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns around higher income levels. Killingsworth 2021 challenged the strict plateau and found continued increases in experienced well-being with log income. The current synthesis is something like: more money helps, but with diminishing marginal returns, and it tends to affect life evaluation / security more than moment-to-moment enjoyment.

So if the OpenAI offer is “a bit higher salary” and you are already highly compensated, the research would usually suggest not overweighting the salary difference unless it materially changes your financial life or sense of fairness.

## 5. Leadership could be intrinsically valuable, not just extrinsic

This is the big nuance in your case. “Leading a small team” is not equivalent to “more status and money.” It can also mean:

- more autonomy  
- more influence over direction  
- more responsibility  
- more mentoring / relatedness  
- more learning  
- more identity fulfillment: “I am becoming the kind of person I wanted to become”  
- more career capital  

All of those are psychologically important. Self-determination theory — Deci, Ryan, Gagné, Van den Broeck and others — emphasizes autonomy, competence, and relatedness as core needs. A leadership role can satisfy all three, if it is well-designed. But it can also reduce deep technical work, increase meetings, increase conflict, and add stress without enough control.

The evidence would therefore push you to ask: is this role “less interesting” because the technical problem is less interesting, or because the entire day-to-day job would be less intrinsically motivating? Those are different.

A less interesting research area plus high autonomy, high mentoring enjoyment, high impact, and strong team fit could still be very satisfying. A less interesting area plus admin, politics, weak technical taste, and little maker time would be risky.

## 6. Social environment and manager quality are huge

Work-design meta-analyses also find large effects for **social support**, supervisor quality, coworker relationships, and psychological safety. In some studies, social support is as predictive of job satisfaction as the task characteristics themselves.

So “interesting work” should not be evaluated in isolation. A fascinating technical problem in a bad social environment can be miserable; a moderately interesting problem with excellent colleagues and autonomy can be great.

For your comparison, the empirical question is not just “Anthropic research vs OpenAI salary/team.” It is more like:

- Which environment gives you more autonomy?  
- Which manager / leadership chain will you trust more?  
- Which team has better psychological safety?  
- Which role gives you more energy at the end of a week?  
- Where will you learn faster?  
- Where will you have more ownership?  
- Which role has a better ratio of deep work to coordination overhead?  

## 7. Regret research: less direct, but useful

There is not much clean research of the form: “People who chose the more interesting job over the higher-paying leadership job later regretted or did not regret it.” But general regret research gives some clues.

Gilovich & Medvec 1995 and later work find that people often regret **inactions** more than actions over the long run. Roese & Summerville 2005 found that common major life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and missed opportunities. Davidai & Gilovich 2018 argue that the most enduring regrets often concern failures to live up to one’s **ideal self** — hopes, dreams, aspirations — more than failures of duty or obligation.

Applied cautiously: if “leading a team” is part of your ideal self, then declining that opportunity could be something you later regret. But if “doing frontier, deeply interesting research” is more central to your ideal self, then leaving it for status/pay could be the regret. The research does not say which identity should dominate; it says regrets are often about personally salient unrealized possibilities.

## My evidence-based read

If you strip away the specific companies, the literature suggests:

1. **Do not dismiss interesting work.** It is one of the most robust predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.  
2. **Do not overweight a modest salary increase**, especially if you are already financially secure.  
3. **Treat leadership as a potentially intrinsic factor**, not merely an extrinsic one. If leading people is genuinely something you value, that is psychologically important.  
4. **Model the actual week**, not the title. The biggest happiness effects usually come from daily activities, autonomy, social environment, stress, and meaning.  
5. **Regret will probably track whichever path feels more like a missed “ideal self” opportunity.**

A practical research-informed test would be to score both roles on:

- intellectual interest  
- autonomy  
- meaning / mission fit  
- skill growth  
- amount of deep work  
- quality of manager / peers  
- stress and workload predictability  
- opportunity to lead  
- future optionality  
- financial impact  

If OpenAI wins mostly on salary and title, while Anthropic wins strongly on interest, meaning, and day-to-day energy, the research would lean toward staying. If OpenAI wins on leadership, autonomy, growth, team quality, and future identity — not just pay — then the research case for moving becomes much stronger.
